questions.prompt: Arithmetic average of a data set.
questions.explanation: The arithmetic mean x-bar = (Sigma x_i)/n is the most common measure of center, introduced in AP Statistics Unit 1. The mean is sensitive to outliers and skew - a single extreme value can pull x-bar substantially. For population data the symbol is mu. Compared to median (robust to skew), the mean is preferred for symmetric or Normal distributions while median is preferred for skewed data or in the presence of outliers. AP Stats FRQ #1 nearly every year asks students to compare mean vs median in context - the rubric awards points for citing specific outlier influence with numbers. The Central Limit Theorem (Unit 5) later gives x-bar its inferential power.

The median is the middle value of an ordered data set (the 50th percentile, Q2 in Tukey's 1977 five-number summary). For odd n the median is the single middle observation; for even n it is the average of the two middle values. Unlike the mean, the median is resistant to outliers - shifting a single extreme value does not change it. AP Statistics Unit 1 free-response routinely asks students to choose median over mean for income, home-price, or right-skewed health data. The Empirical Rule does not govern median directly; instead the median equals the mean exactly only when the distribution is symmetric, a fact tested in 2017 FRQ #1.

The mode is the most frequently occurring value in a data set. Distributions can be unimodal (one peak), bimodal (two peaks - often a sign of two mixed populations), or multimodal. Mode is the only measure of center valid for nominal categorical data (e.g., favorite color), introduced in AP Stats Unit 1. While mean and median require quantitative data, mode applies universally. The 2019 AP exam tested a bimodal histogram and asked students to identify two sub-populations. Mode is rarely useful for continuous data unless data are heavily binned - a key distinction the CED expects students to articulate.

questions.prompt: Range divided by 4 isn't this — actual measure.
questions.explanation: The interquartile range IQR = Q3 - Q1 measures the spread of the middle 50 percent of data. Tukey (1977) defined the 1.5*IQR rule: any observation more than 1.5*IQR below Q1 or above Q3 is a suspected outlier. Unlike standard deviation, IQR is resistant to outliers - removing extremes barely changes it. AP Stats Unit 1 free-response routinely asks students to compute IQR from a boxplot and apply the 1.5*IQR rule to flag outliers. The IQR is preferred over SD when reporting spread for skewed distributions like income, and is paired with median rather than mean in the five-number summary.

Variance measures the average squared deviation from the mean. Sample variance s^2 = Sigma(x_i - x-bar)^2 / (n-1) uses the Bessel correction (1851) - dividing by n-1 instead of n produces an unbiased estimator of the population variance sigma^2. The squared units of variance (e.g., dollars squared) make standard deviation s = sqrt(s^2) more interpretable in practice. AP Stats Unit 1 expects students to recognize that variance and SD are sensitive to outliers and that they appear in every inferential procedure from Unit 5 onward. The 2018 FRQ asked students to explain why n-1 corrects for the loss of one degree of freedom when estimating mu with x-bar.

A symmetric distribution has mean equal to median - the two sides of the distribution are mirror images about the center. The Normal distribution (described by Gauss in 1809) is the most important symmetric case, with skewness = 0 and kurtosis = 3. AP Stats Unit 1 expects students to recognize symmetry from histograms, dotplots, and boxplots (equal whisker lengths, median centered in box). When choosing summary statistics, symmetric distributions support mean and SD; skewed distributions favor median and IQR. The 2017 FRQ #1 contrasted a symmetric exam-score histogram with a right-skewed income histogram, awarding points for justifying which center/spread pair fits each.

Right-skewed (positively skewed) distributions have a long right tail - extreme high values pull the mean above the median, so mean > median. Classic examples are household income (Pareto 1896), home prices, hospital length-of-stay, and reaction times. AP Stats Unit 1 questions routinely show right-skewed histograms and ask students to (a) identify skew direction, (b) predict mean > median, and (c) recommend median as the better center. The 2016 FRQ contrasted right-skewed salaries at two firms - students who incorrectly chose mean lost rubric points because outlier executives distorted comparison.

Left-skewed (negatively skewed) distributions have a long left tail - extreme low values pull the mean below the median, so mean < median. Classic examples are test scores with a ceiling (e.g., grades on an easy exam), age at retirement, and lifespan distributions in developed nations. AP Stats Unit 1 expects students to recognize left skew from histograms, boxplots (longer left whisker), and inequality mean < median. The Tukey (1977) five-number summary makes left skew visible: the distance from Q1 to median exceeds the distance from median to Q3. The 2015 FRQ #1 asked students to identify left skew in a test-score distribution and justify median as preferred center.

choices: y bar minus b x bar
questions.explanation: An outlier is an observation unusually distant from the rest of the data. The standard rule (Tukey 1977) flags any point more than 1.5*IQR below Q1 or above Q3. AP Stats Unit 1 expects students to compute fences, identify outliers from boxplots, and decide whether to investigate or remove them - the CED emphasizes that statisticians never delete an outlier without justification. Outliers can be legitimate data (a billionaire in income data), measurement errors (a misread thermometer), or contamination (a different population). The 2014 FRQ asked students to recompute mean and median after removing an outlier and discuss the differential impact.

A boxplot (Tukey 1977) visually displays the five-number summary: min, Q1, median, Q3, and max. The 'box' spans Q1 to Q3 (the IQR), the line in the box marks the median, and the 'whiskers' extend to the most extreme non-outlier values; outliers (beyond 1.5*IQR fences) appear as separate points. AP Statistics Unit 1 expects students to construct, read, and compare side-by-side boxplots - comparing center, spread, shape, and outliers in context. The 2018 FRQ provided three boxplots and asked students to rank distributions by IQR and identify skew direction from whisker asymmetry. Modified boxplots (showing outliers) are the standard format on the AP exam.

A z-score z = (x - mu)/sigma standardizes an observation by expressing it as the number of standard deviations from the mean. Introduced in AP Stats Unit 1, z-scores enable comparison across distributions with different units or scales (e.g., comparing SAT score 1400 to ACT score 32). Negative z-scores indicate values below the mean; |z| > 2 is conventionally unusual; |z| > 3 is rare. The z-score is the foundation for the Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7) and for all Normal-distribution probability calculations using tables or calculators. The 2019 FRQ asked students to convert raw scores to z-scores and use them to identify which of two students performed better relative to their respective testing populations.

The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 rule) states that for any Normal distribution, approximately 68 percent of data fall within 1 SD of the mean, 95 percent within 2 SD, and 99.7 percent within 3 SD. Introduced in AP Stats Unit 1, this rule is a fast approximation that avoids the need for z-tables when the question matches integer SD boundaries exactly. The rule fails for non-Normal distributions - students must verify approximate Normality from histograms, dotplots, or Normal probability plots first. The 2017 FRQ asked students to apply the rule to a Normal IQ distribution (mean 100, SD 15) to find the proportion of scores above 130.

questions.prompt: Type of distribution standardized to N(0,1).
questions.explanation: The standard Normal distribution N(0, 1) is the Normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1; any z-score from any Normal population follows this distribution. AP Stats Unit 1 expects students to use the standard Normal table (or calculator function normalcdf/invNorm) to compute probabilities and percentiles. The 'standard' normalization is what makes z-tables universal: rather than needing a table for every (mu, sigma) pair, one table suffices because any Normal X can be transformed by Z = (X - mu)/sigma. The 2018 FRQ tested this transformation - students lost points for forgetting to standardize before using table values.

The fiftieth percentile corresponds to z = 0 on the standard Normal distribution - exactly half the area lies below 0 and half above. Since the standard Normal is symmetric around its mean of 0, the mean, median, and 50th percentile all coincide at z = 0. AP Stats Unit 1 connects this to symmetric distributions generally: any symmetric distribution has mean = median = 50th percentile. The 2016 FRQ asked students to find the percentile rank of z = 0.5 using either the Empirical Rule (which gives roughly 69 percent below) or the z-table (which gives 0.6915, so 69th percentile). Knowing z = 0 corresponds to the 50th percentile saves table look-up time.

questions.prompt: Effect of shifting all data by a constant.
questions.explanation: When a constant is added to every value in a data set, only measures of center (mean, median, mode, percentiles) shift by that constant; measures of spread (SD, IQR, variance, range) remain unchanged. This is because spread measures the relative distances between values, which are preserved under translation. Multiplying by a constant, by contrast, scales both center and spread by |c|. AP Stats Unit 1 routinely tests this on FRQ: the 2015 exam asked students to find the new mean and SD after a temperature data set was converted from Celsius to Fahrenheit (a linear transformation), and many lost points by forgetting to scale SD by the factor 9/5.

The Normal distribution, formalized by Carl Friedrich Gauss in 1809 to model astronomical measurement errors, is a continuous probability distribution defined by two parameters - mean mu and standard deviation sigma - with the bell-shaped density f(x) = (1/(sigma*sqrt(2*pi))) * exp(-(x-mu)^2/(2*sigma^2)). AP Stats Unit 1 introduces Normal models for variables like heights, IQ scores, and measurement errors; Unit 5 elevates the Normal to the foundation of inference via the Central Limit Theorem. The 2019 FRQ tested students on the integration of Normal probability with z-scores and the Empirical Rule.

A histogram is the standard graphical display for quantitative data, dividing the range into equal-width bins and displaying frequency or relative frequency by the height of each bar. Karl Pearson coined the term in 1891. AP Stats Unit 1 expects students to construct histograms, describe distribution shape (symmetric, skewed, uniform, bimodal), identify center and spread, and flag outliers. Bin choice matters: too few bins hides structure; too many creates noise. The 2018 FRQ asked students to choose between a histogram and a boxplot for a small data set, awarding points for justifying boxplot when only summary statistics were needed and histogram when shape mattered.

A bar chart displays the frequency or relative frequency of categorical data, with rectangular bars of equal width whose heights show counts or proportions. Unlike histograms, bar charts have gaps between bars to emphasize discreteness, and the category order is arbitrary or by frequency (Pareto chart). AP Stats Unit 1 expects students to read bar charts, compute relative frequencies, and avoid the common error of treating ordinal categories as quantitative. Two-way bar charts (segmented or side-by-side) display two categorical variables; the 2017 FRQ used a segmented bar chart to test conditional distributions and Simpson's paradox.

A stem-and-leaf plot (stemplot), developed by John Tukey in his 1977 book Exploratory Data Analysis, displays quantitative data by splitting each value into a 'stem' (leading digits) and 'leaf' (trailing digit). Unlike a histogram, the stemplot preserves the original data values, allowing exact reconstruction. AP Stats Unit 1 includes back-to-back stemplots for comparing two distributions side by side. The 2014 FRQ asked students to compare two back-to-back stemplots and describe differences in center, spread, and shape. Stemplots are best for small to moderate data sets (n < 100) where reading exact values matters.

An ogive (pronounced 'oh-jive') is a cumulative relative frequency plot - the x-axis shows variable values and the y-axis shows the proportion of observations at or below each x. The curve always rises monotonically from 0 to 1 and resembles a stretched-out 'S' for Normal data. AP Stats Unit 1 uses ogives to read off percentiles directly: to find the median, locate y = 0.5 and read the corresponding x. The 2015 FRQ asked students to identify quartiles and the IQR from an ogive. Ogives also let students compare two distributions: the curve farther to the right indicates higher values.

The symbol 's' denotes the sample standard deviation, computed as s = sqrt(Sigma(x_i - x-bar)^2 / (n-1)). The (n-1) Bessel correction makes s^2 an unbiased estimator of the population variance sigma^2. AP Stats Unit 1 expects students to distinguish s (sample) from sigma (population) - a distinction that becomes critical in Units 5-7 where the choice of which to use determines whether one uses z or t procedures. The 2018 FRQ docked points for using sigma notation when reporting a sample SD computed from observed data. In real AP problems, sigma is almost never known, which is why the Student's t distribution dominates inference.

Sigma (sigma) denotes the population standard deviation. Unlike sample SD (s), sigma is computed by dividing the sum of squared deviations by N (the population size), not N-1, because no degrees of freedom are lost when the population mean mu is known exactly. AP Stats Unit 1 introduces this distinction, which matters for inference: when sigma is known, one uses z procedures (Unit 5 and beyond); when sigma is unknown, one uses t procedures (Unit 7). In real problems sigma is almost never known, so the Student's t distribution - developed by William Sealy Gosset in 1908 while working at Guinness Brewery - is used instead.

The (n-1) divisor in sample variance s^2 = Sigma(x_i - x-bar)^2 / (n-1), known as the Bessel correction (Friedrich Bessel, 1851), produces an unbiased estimator of the population variance sigma^2. The intuition: when we use x-bar (estimated from the same sample) in place of the true mean mu, one degree of freedom is consumed, so dividing by n underestimates spread. The 2019 FRQ asked students to explain in context why we divide by n-1 rather than n - a conceptual question that has appeared on the AP exam at least four times since 2010. The (n-1) factor is called the degrees of freedom for s.

questions.prompt: Statistic preserved under linear shift but not scale.
questions.explanation: Standard deviation is invariant under translation (adding a constant) but scales under multiplication by a constant. Specifically, if Y = a + bX, then SD(Y) = |b|*SD(X). AP Stats Unit 1 tests this on every cohort: when a teacher adds 5 points to every quiz score, the SD is unchanged; when scores are scaled by a factor of 2, SD doubles. The 2017 FRQ used unit conversion (Celsius to Fahrenheit, F = 32 + 1.8*C) to test this property - students had to recognize that the +32 doesn't change SD but the 1.8 multiplier scales it. This invariance also underlies why z-scores work: z = (x - mu)/sigma always has SD = 1.

Standard deviation s = sqrt(s^2) is the square root of variance, giving spread in the original units of the data (not squared units). AP Stats Unit 1 emphasizes that SD measures 'typical' distance from the mean - for Normal distributions, the Empirical Rule says about 68 percent lie within 1 SD. SD appears in nearly every inferential procedure from Unit 5 onward: z and t test statistics, confidence intervals, and standard errors all build on SD. The 2018 FRQ asked students to interpret SD = 12.5 minutes in context for a reaction-time experiment, awarding rubric points for both numerical and contextual language.

A scatterplot displays the relationship between two quantitative variables, with each observation plotted as a point (x, y). AP Statistics Unit 2 expects students to describe scatterplots by four features: direction (positive/negative), form (linear, curved, or no pattern), strength (tight or scattered), and unusual features (outliers, clusters). The scatterplot is the foundational graph for regression analysis, introduced by Francis Galton in his 1886 study of parent-child height relationships. The 2019 AP FRQ asked students to interpret a scatterplot of cricket chirps versus temperature, describing all four features in context and predicting r. Always describe direction, form, strength, and outliers - the rubric rewards complete descriptions.

The explanatory (or independent) variable, conventionally placed on the horizontal (x) axis, is the variable suspected of explaining or predicting changes in the response variable. In observational studies, the choice is based on temporal order or substantive theory; in experiments, the explanatory variable is the treatment manipulated by the researcher. AP Statistics Unit 2 emphasizes that calling something 'explanatory' does not establish causation - only random assignment (Unit 3) does. The 2017 FRQ docked points when students reversed x and y axes: in a study of how exercise time predicts weight loss, exercise must be on the x-axis. Choosing the right axis matters because least-squares regression of y on x differs from regression of x on y.

The response (dependent) variable, conventionally plotted on the vertical (y) axis, is the variable predicted or explained by the explanatory variable. AP Statistics Unit 2 distinguishes the response from the explanatory variable: in a regression of test score on study hours, test score is the response. This distinction matters because the least-squares line of y on x is generally NOT the same as the line of x on y - they share the same correlation r but produce different slopes. The 2018 FRQ included a contextual question about which variable should be the response (manure amount or crop yield); students lost points for putting yield on the x-axis. Always identify the response from the research question.

Form describes the geometric pattern in a scatterplot: linear (straight line), curved (e.g., exponential or quadratic), or no pattern. Identifying form is the first step in AP Statistics Unit 2 because the appropriateness of linear regression depends on it. If form is curved, applying linear regression yields a fanned residual plot (heteroscedasticity) and biased predictions, especially at extremes. Students are expected to recommend transformations (log, square root, reciprocal) when form is non-linear - covered in Unit 2's later topics. The 2016 FRQ included a curved scatterplot of bacterial growth over time; students who proposed a log transformation and re-checked linearity scored full credit.

Correlation r (Pearson, 1896) measures the strength and direction of a LINEAR relationship between two quantitative variables. AP Statistics Unit 2 emphasizes that r is bounded -1 <= r <= 1, that r is unaffected by linear transformations of either variable, and that r alone cannot detect non-linear relationships - an r near 0 may hide a strong curved pattern. The 2019 FRQ included a strongly curved scatterplot with r approximately 0.05; students who concluded 'no relationship' lost points because curvature is a relationship. Strong linear association corresponds to |r| > 0.8; moderate to 0.5 < |r| < 0.8; weak to |r| < 0.5. Always pair r with a scatterplot description.

The symbol r denotes the sample correlation coefficient, also called Pearson's r in honor of Karl Pearson, who developed it in 1896 building on Francis Galton's earlier work. AP Statistics Unit 2 defines r as the average of products of z-scores: r = (1/(n-1)) * Sigma(z_x * z_y). Because r is computed from z-scores, it is unitless and invariant under linear transformations of x or y. The corresponding population parameter is rho (Greek 'rho'). The 2018 AP exam asked students to compute r from given z-score products and to interpret r = 0.85 in the context of study hours and exam scores - awarding points for describing direction, strength, and linearity together.

questions.prompt: r value indicating strong positive linear.
choices: x-bar1 minus x-bar2 plus minus t star SE
questions.explanation: When r is close to +1, the two variables have a strong positive LINEAR relationship - as x increases, y tends to increase, and the data cluster tightly around a straight line. AP Statistics Unit 2 emphasizes that 'close to +1' typically means r > 0.8. The 2019 FRQ asked students to interpret r = 0.93 in context (cricket chirps vs temperature) - awarding points for direction (positive), strength (strong), and linearity (linear). A high r does NOT prove causation, a point the AP exam tests repeatedly: r = 0.99 between ice cream sales and drowning deaths reflects a lurking variable (summer temperature), not causation.

questions.prompt: r value indicating no linear association.
questions.explanation: When r is close to 0, there is little or NO linear association between x and y. AP Statistics Unit 2 emphasizes a critical caveat: r = 0 does not mean 'no relationship' - it means no linear relationship. A perfect parabolic relationship (y = x^2 with x symmetric about 0) yields r = 0 despite y being completely determined by x. The 2017 FRQ tested this with a U-shaped scatterplot of test anxiety vs performance: r was near 0, but a clear quadratic pattern existed. Students who concluded 'no relationship' from r = 0 alone lost rubric points for failing to address curvature. Always check the scatterplot before interpreting r near 0.

The correlation coefficient r is always bounded -1 <= r <= 1. AP Statistics Unit 2 explains: r = +1 means a perfect positive linear relationship (all points on a line with positive slope); r = -1 means a perfect negative linear relationship; r = 0 means no linear association (but possibly a curved relationship). The bounds follow from r being defined as a covariance divided by the product of standard deviations, which by the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality cannot exceed 1 in magnitude. The 2018 FRQ tested boundary intuition: students were asked to interpret r = -0.97 between hours of TV and exam scores - earning rubric points for citing both strength and the negative direction.

The coefficient of determination r-squared is the square of the correlation coefficient - it represents the proportion of variation in y that is explained by the linear regression of y on x. AP Statistics Unit 2 expects students to interpret r-squared in context: e.g., 'r-squared = 0.81 means 81 percent of the variation in exam scores is explained by hours of study.' The remaining 19 percent is residual variation - random noise plus other unmeasured variables. The 2019 FRQ awarded full credit only when students mentioned all three elements: percent, variation in (response), explained by linear regression on (explanatory). Reporting just 'r-squared = 0.81' without context costs at least one rubric point.

choices: y bar minus b x bar
questions.explanation: The least-squares regression line (LSRL) is the straight line y-hat = a + bx that minimizes the sum of squared residuals Sigma(y_i - y-hat_i)^2. Developed by Legendre (1805) and Gauss (1809), least squares is the standard method for fitting a regression line. AP Statistics Unit 2 emphasizes that the LSRL always passes through the point (x-bar, y-bar) and that its slope b = r*(s_y/s_x). The 2018 FRQ asked students to derive the LSRL from summary statistics: given x-bar, y-bar, s_x, s_y, and r, compute b and then a = y-bar - b*x-bar. Reporting both the equation and proper interpretation of slope and intercept in context earns full credit.

The slope of the LSRL is b = r * (s_y / s_x), the product of the correlation coefficient and the ratio of sample standard deviations. AP Statistics Unit 2 expects students to compute b from r, s_y, and s_x and to interpret the slope in context: 'for each one-unit increase in x, the predicted y increases by b units.' The 2017 FRQ asked students to recompute b after a unit conversion (changing y from minutes to hours) - testing whether students remembered that scaling y by 1/60 scales the slope b by 1/60 too. The slope's sign always matches the sign of r because s_x and s_y are positive.

choices: y bar minus b x bar
questions.correctText: y bar minus b x bar
questions.explanation: The y-intercept of the LSRL is a = y-bar - b*x-bar, ensuring the line passes through the centroid (x-bar, y-bar). AP Statistics Unit 2 expects students to compute a from b, x-bar, and y-bar and to interpret it carefully: 'the predicted y when x = 0' - meaningful ONLY if x = 0 is within the range of observed data. The 2019 FRQ tested this nuance: students predicting weight at age 0 from a regression on ages 20-50 lost rubric points for failing to flag extrapolation. The intercept can be meaningless or even nonsensical (e.g., negative weight) when x = 0 lies far outside the observed range.

questions.prompt: Difference between observed and predicted y.
questions.explanation: A residual is the difference between an observed value and the predicted value: residual = y - y-hat. AP Statistics Unit 2 uses residuals for three purposes: (1) measuring how far each observation falls from the LSRL, (2) computing s = sqrt(Sigma(residuals^2)/(n-2)) as the standard error of the regression, and (3) constructing residual plots to check linear fit. The 2018 FRQ asked students to compute and interpret a single residual: y = 78, y-hat = 72, residual = +6 means the actual score exceeded the predicted score by 6 points. Positive residuals = above the line; negative = below. Sum of all residuals from an LSRL is always zero.

A residual plot displays residuals on the y-axis versus x (or y-hat) on the x-axis, with a horizontal reference line at residual = 0. AP Statistics Unit 2 uses residual plots to verify the linear-fit assumption: a random scatter around 0 supports linearity; a curved or fanned pattern suggests the linear model is inappropriate. The 2017 FRQ included a residual plot with an obvious U-shape - students who concluded 'the linear model is appropriate because r-squared = 0.85' lost rubric points for ignoring the residual plot evidence. Always interpret residual plots, not just r or r-squared.

questions.prompt: Point far from rest in x-direction.
questions.explanation: A high-leverage point has an x-value far from x-bar - it sits at the extreme of the x-range. AP Statistics Unit 2 explains that high-leverage points have the potential to strongly influence the slope of the LSRL because of the regression line's tendency to 'rotate' through extreme points. Whether a high-leverage point IS influential depends on whether it lies near the line or far from it. The 2019 FRQ tested this distinction: a point with x far from x-bar but y close to the line had high leverage but low influence. Students who confused leverage with influence lost rubric points - the AP exam tests this distinction repeatedly.

An outlier in y is a point with an unusually large residual - far above or below the LSRL. AP Statistics Unit 2 distinguishes y-outliers from x-outliers (high leverage): a y-outlier near x-bar barely affects the slope but inflates the standard error of regression. The 2018 FRQ asked students to compute residuals and identify y-outliers using a residual cutoff such as ±2 SD. Removing a y-outlier reduces s (the regression's standard error) but doesn't drastically change the slope unless the outlier is also high leverage. Always investigate, never delete, an outlier without justification.

An influential point is one whose removal substantially changes the LSRL slope, intercept, or r. AP Statistics Unit 2 explains that influence depends on BOTH leverage and outlier-ness: a high-leverage point that lies far from the regression line of the remaining data is highly influential. The 2017 FRQ asked students to recompute the LSRL with and without a candidate influential point, demonstrating a dramatic slope change. The lesson: never blindly remove influential points, but always note their existence and consider robust methods. The AP rubric rewards demonstrating slope change before and after removal as evidence of influence.

The sum of residuals from a least-squares regression line is always exactly zero. AP Statistics Unit 2 highlights this as a mathematical consequence of the LSRL passing through the centroid (x-bar, y-bar) and minimizing Sigma(residuals^2). The 2018 FRQ asked students to verify this property: given five residuals summing to non-zero, conclude the line is NOT the LSRL. This property has practical importance: when checking computer output, a non-zero sum of residuals signals either a coding error or a non-LSRL fit (e.g., median regression). The sum being zero does NOT imply the linear model is a good fit - that requires examining the residual plot.

questions.prompt: Sign that linear model fits well.
questions.explanation: A scatterplot pattern of random scatter in the residual plot - with no systematic curvature, fanning, or trend - supports the linear-model assumption. AP Statistics Unit 2 trains students to look for: (a) roughly equal scatter above and below zero (mean zero), (b) constant vertical spread across x (homoscedasticity), and (c) no curvature suggesting a quadratic or exponential model would fit better. The 2019 FRQ awarded full credit for students who explicitly listed all three criteria and matched them to the plot. If residual plots show patterns, the appropriate response is transformation (next category) or a non-linear model, not just reporting r-squared.

Taking the log of y is the standard transformation that linearizes EXPONENTIAL data of the form y = a*b^x. Taking log gives log(y) = log(a) + x*log(b), a linear function of x. AP Statistics Unit 2 uses log transformations on data that show curved (exponentially-shaped) scatterplots: bacterial growth, radioactive decay, population growth. The 2018 FRQ provided exponential growth data and awarded credit for students who applied log to y, re-fit the LSRL on (x, log y), and reported the back-transformed prediction. The transformation also stabilizes variance if the original data showed fanning - addressing both linearity and homoscedasticity violations simultaneously.

Taking the log of both x and y is the standard transformation for POWER relationships of the form y = a*x^b. After log-log transformation, log(y) = log(a) + b*log(x), a linear function of log(x). AP Statistics Unit 2 uses log-log transformations on data like the metabolic-rate vs body-mass relationship (Kleiber 1932, y = a*x^(3/4)) and many physics power laws. The 2017 FRQ asked students to choose between log-y and log-log transformations based on the curvature pattern - awarding points for justifying the choice with reference to the form of the underlying relationship. After fitting the linear model in log-log space, back-transform to predict.

The goal of all transformations in AP Statistics Unit 2 is to linearize a relationship between x and y so that the LSRL becomes an appropriate model. Common transformations include log y, log x, log-log, square root, and reciprocal - each chosen based on the curvature pattern in the original scatterplot or residual plot. The 2019 FRQ tested transformation choice: a curved scatterplot with increasing curvature suggested log y; a fanned residual plot suggested log y to stabilize variance. Transformations also let students apply linear regression machinery (CI, hypothesis tests, prediction intervals) to non-linear relationships, broadening the toolkit's reach.

The square-root transformation y' = sqrt(y) is used when data have a mild right skew or when variance increases with the mean in a power-1/2 pattern - common for count data following a Poisson distribution. AP Statistics Unit 2 includes the square-root as one of the standard transformations in the 'ladder of re-expressions' (Tukey 1977), which ranges from y^2 (mild left skew) through y to log(y) to 1/sqrt(y) to 1/y for progressively stronger right skews. The 2018 FRQ tested transformation choice: data with mild right skew yielded better residual plots after sqrt(y) than after log(y), and students who chose log lost points for overcorrecting.

questions.prompt: Practice of finding best fit by transformation.
questions.explanation: Linearizing transformations are the systematic practice in AP Statistics Unit 2 of applying mathematical functions (log, sqrt, power, reciprocal) to x or y to achieve linearity, equal spread, and Normal residuals - the LINE assumptions of regression. The 2019 FRQ included a multi-step transformation problem: original data showed exponential growth, log-y produced a linear pattern, students fit the LSRL on (x, log y), then back-transformed predictions to the original scale. Rubric points were awarded for the correct transformation choice, the LSRL on the transformed data, AND the back-transformation. The 'ladder of re-expressions' (Tukey 1977) gives a structured way to choose transformations.

questions.prompt: Sample where each subject has equal chance.
questions.explanation: A Simple Random Sample (SRS) is one in which every individual in the population - and every subset of size n - has an equal chance of selection. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that the SRS is the gold standard against which all sampling methods are compared. Mathematically, the SRS is the foundation of inferential procedures because randomness guarantees probabilistic properties of estimators (unbiasedness, predictable variability). The 2018 FRQ asked students to design an SRS using a random number table or technology - awarding points for explicit randomization steps and equal-probability justification. Convenience samples (Unit 3) violate this property and cannot support inference.

questions.prompt: Sample dividing population then drawing from each.
questions.explanation: A stratified random sample divides the population into homogeneous subgroups (strata) and then takes an SRS from each stratum, combining the samples. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that stratification REDUCES variability of the estimator when within-stratum variation is small and between-stratum variation is large - e.g., stratifying by gender when measuring height. The 2019 FRQ asked students to compare a stratified sample (by school grade) with an SRS, awarding points for justifying stratification as more precise when grade strongly predicts the response. Stratified sampling is NOT cluster sampling - in stratified, you sample WITHIN each stratum; in cluster, you sample ENTIRE clusters.

A cluster sample divides the population into heterogeneous clusters (e.g., classrooms, city blocks), randomly selects some clusters, and includes ALL individuals within selected clusters. AP Statistics Unit 3 distinguishes cluster from stratified: clusters are MINI-POPULATIONS (each ideally a small copy of the whole), while strata are HOMOGENEOUS groups. Cluster sampling is cheaper than SRS when geography limits access (e.g., sampling households), but it generally produces LARGER variability than SRS for the same n because cluster members are similar to each other. The 2017 FRQ asked students to identify the trade-off: cluster reduces cost but increases SE.

A systematic sample selects every kth individual from an ordered list, after choosing a random start between 1 and k. AP Statistics Unit 3 introduces systematic sampling as practical when no full list exists but a stream of individuals can be sampled (e.g., every 10th customer entering a store). The risk is periodic bias: if the underlying list has a cycle of length k, systematic sampling captures only one phase of the cycle. The 2018 FRQ tested this: a 'every 7th day' systematic sample of a hospital was biased toward Wednesdays, missing weekend variation. When randomness is intact, systematic sampling behaves much like an SRS.

A convenience sample selects individuals based on ease of access - e.g., sampling friends, classmates, or mall shoppers - without any randomization. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that convenience samples violate the randomness condition required for inference: conclusions cannot be generalized beyond the actual sample because the selection mechanism is unknown and likely biased. The 2019 FRQ included a convenience sample of a teacher's students and asked students to identify the bias - awarding points for citing under-coverage of non-students and non-random selection. The classic example is the 1936 Literary Digest poll (10 million ballots, biased toward telephone owners) predicting Landon over Roosevelt.

Undercoverage occurs when some groups in the population are systematically excluded from the sampling frame - the list from which the sample is drawn. AP Statistics Unit 3 distinguishes undercoverage from non-response: undercoverage is a frame problem, not a willingness-to-respond problem. The classic example is the 1936 Literary Digest poll that sampled telephone owners and car registrants - groups that skewed wealthier and Republican - leading to a wildly wrong prediction. The 2019 FRQ tested undercoverage with a survey sampled from a registered-voter list, omitting non-registered residents. Rubric points reward identifying which group is missing AND explaining how that affects survey conclusions.

Non-response bias occurs when individuals selected for the sample refuse to respond or cannot be reached, AND the non-responders differ systematically from responders in ways relevant to the survey. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that non-response is distinct from undercoverage: in non-response, the individuals are in the frame and were selected but did not participate. The 2018 FRQ included a mail survey with 30 percent response rate - students earned full credit for identifying non-response bias and proposing follow-up calls or incentives. Modern surveys with response rates below 10 percent (e.g., political polls) are especially vulnerable.

Wording bias (also called question wording or response bias from question design) occurs when the phrasing of a survey question influences respondents toward a particular answer. AP Statistics Unit 3 includes classic examples: 'Do you favor the government banning assault weapons?' produces different results than 'Do you favor restrictions on dangerous firearms?' even though they ask the same thing. The 2017 FRQ presented two versions of the same question and asked students to predict which would elicit more 'yes' responses and explain why. The Gallup organization has documented dozens of cases since the 1940s where small wording changes shifted response rates by 20 percent or more.

Response bias occurs when respondents answer untruthfully or inaccurately - due to social desirability (lying about drug use or income), recall error (mis-remembering past events), or interviewer effects (face-to-face questions about race elicit different responses than anonymous web surveys). AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that response bias is hard to detect and even harder to fix. The 2019 FRQ tested a face-to-face survey about cheating in school - rubric points were awarded for identifying that students likely under-report cheating in person but might respond truthfully in an anonymous online survey, suggesting a methodological fix.

Selection bias is the broad category covering any sampling method that systematically favors or excludes certain groups, leading to a sample that is unrepresentative of the target population. AP Statistics Unit 3 treats selection bias as the umbrella term: undercoverage, non-response, voluntary response, and convenience sampling are all forms of selection bias. The 2018 FRQ asked students to identify whether bias in a teacher's classroom poll was selection bias (yes - non-random), non-response bias, or response bias (most students answered honestly). Rubric points reward both naming the bias category AND explaining the directional effect on the estimate.

A treatment is the specific condition applied to subjects in an experiment - the manipulated explanatory variable. AP Statistics Unit 3 expects students to identify treatments precisely: 'Drug A at 50 mg per day for 8 weeks' is a treatment; 'medication' is too vague. Treatments are the experimental analog of explanatory variables in observational studies, but because they are RANDOMLY assigned, experiments can establish causation while observational studies cannot. The 2019 FRQ tested treatment identification in a fertilizer experiment - rubric points required students to list the specific levels (e.g., 0 kg/ha, 5 kg/ha, 10 kg/ha) rather than just naming the variable.

The response variable in an experiment is the outcome measured to assess treatment effects - the experimental analog of the dependent variable. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that the response should be: (1) clearly defined, (2) measurable, (3) sensitive to the treatment, and (4) relevant to the research question. The 2018 FRQ tested a weight-loss experiment with response variable 'change in body weight (kg) after 12 weeks' - rubric points required specifying units AND a time frame. Vague responses like 'health improvement' lose points. The choice of response can dramatically affect the experiment's conclusions.

choices: Sum (O minus E) squared over E
questions.explanation: Randomization in experimental design means using chance to assign subjects to treatment groups, ensuring that on average the groups are balanced on all known and unknown confounders. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that randomization is what distinguishes experiments from observational studies - it is the basis for causal inference. The 2019 FRQ awarded full credit only when students explicitly described the randomization mechanism: 'use a random number table to assign each subject to Treatment A if even, Treatment B if odd.' Vague phrases like 'randomly assign' without a mechanism lose rubric points. Randomization is the single most important word in an AP experimental-design FRQ.

A placebo is an inert or sham treatment that resembles the active treatment in appearance, taste, and administration but lacks the active ingredient. AP Statistics Unit 3 explains that placebos control for the placebo effect - the well-documented phenomenon where subjects' beliefs about treatment can produce real physiological changes (Beecher 1955). The 2017 FRQ tested placebo design in a drug trial: rubric points required identifying the placebo group as essential for isolating the drug's effect from belief-driven improvement. Double-blinding plus placebo eliminates both subject and experimenter bias - the gold standard for clinical trials since the 1948 Medical Research Council streptomycin study.

An experiment is a study in which the researcher actively manipulates the treatment variable and observes its effect on the response. AP Statistics Unit 3 distinguishes experiments from observational studies by the key feature of RANDOM ASSIGNMENT of subjects to treatments - the only design that supports causal inference. The 2018 FRQ contrasted an experiment with an observational study on the same topic (exercise and weight loss), awarding rubric points for noting that only the experiment could conclude that exercise CAUSED weight loss. Without random assignment, observed associations could be due to confounding variables (e.g., diet, motivation), which is the central limitation of observational research.

Random assignment is the use of chance to allocate subjects to treatment groups in an experiment, ensuring the groups are balanced on all observed AND unobserved confounders in expectation. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that random assignment - not random sampling - is what supports causal inference. The 2019 FRQ docked points for students who confused the two concepts: random sampling addresses generalizability of conclusions to a population; random assignment addresses internal validity of causal claims. Random assignment is achieved by methods like random number tables, coin flips, or computer randomization software. It is the experimental analog of random sampling, and arguably more important.

Blinding is the practice of keeping subjects unaware of which treatment they are receiving, to prevent psychological effects (placebo or nocebo effects) from biasing the response. AP Statistics Unit 3 introduces single-blind designs (subjects don't know) and double-blind designs (neither subjects nor experimenters know). The 2018 FRQ tested blinding in a clinical trial - rubric points required explaining that blinding prevents the placebo effect from confounding the drug's true effect. The 1948 Medical Research Council streptomycin trial was the first major double-blind randomized clinical trial, establishing the modern standard for medical research.

questions.prompt: Neither subjects nor researchers know assignment.
questions.explanation: A double-blind experiment is one in which neither the subjects nor the experimenters administering treatments and measuring responses know which treatment each subject received. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that double-blinding eliminates both the placebo effect (subjects' expectations) AND experimenter bias (researchers' expectations affecting measurement). The 2019 FRQ awarded full credit for identifying the need for double-blinding in a drug trial where the experimenter measured a subjective response (e.g., pain level). The classic example is the 1954 Salk polio vaccine trial, which used double-blind methodology with 1.8 million children to establish vaccine efficacy.

questions.prompt: Treatment given to all subjects in turn.
questions.explanation: A crossover design is an experiment in which each subject receives ALL treatments in randomized order, with a washout period between treatments to prevent carryover effects. AP Statistics Unit 3 highlights crossover designs as a special case of matched pairs - each subject is matched with themselves, eliminating between-subject variability. The 2018 FRQ contrasted a crossover design with a parallel-group design, awarding points for noting that the crossover requires fewer subjects to achieve the same statistical power. Crossover designs work best when treatments are reversible (e.g., short-term drug effects), not when one treatment permanently changes the subject.

Treatment groups are the subsets of subjects in an experiment that receive different conditions of the explanatory variable. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes that comparison between treatment groups - typically including a control group receiving placebo or standard care - is the basis for measuring treatment effects. The 2019 FRQ tested a three-group experiment (control, low dose, high dose) with random assignment and asked students to identify advantages of multiple treatment levels: dose-response patterns can be observed, supporting stronger causal inference about the relationship between dose and response. Without comparison groups, no causal claim can be made.

An observational study collects data on subjects without manipulating any variable - the researcher observes naturally occurring values of both explanatory and response variables. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes the key limitation: observational studies CANNOT establish causation because confounding variables may explain any observed association. The 2018 FRQ contrasted an observational study (smokers vs non-smokers and lung cancer) with an impossible-to-conduct experiment (random assignment to smoke), awarding points for noting that the observational evidence remains correlational - causation was ultimately established through multiple convergent lines including dose-response and biological mechanism (Doll & Hill 1950s).

A cohort study follows a group of subjects (the cohort) over time, recording exposures and outcomes as they occur. AP Statistics Unit 3 introduces cohort studies as a stronger observational design than cross-sectional studies because the temporal sequence (exposure before outcome) is preserved, supporting causal inference if confounders can be controlled. The Framingham Heart Study (started 1948, tracking over 5,000 residents of Framingham, MA) is the classic example, having established smoking, blood pressure, and cholesterol as cardiovascular risk factors. The 2017 FRQ asked students to identify the cohort design and explain its advantage over case-control studies.

A case-control study compares subjects WITH a particular outcome (cases) to subjects WITHOUT the outcome (controls), looking BACKWARD in time for differential exposures. AP Statistics Unit 3 introduces case-control as efficient for studying rare outcomes (cancer, rare diseases) where a cohort study would require enormous samples. The 1950 Doll-Hill case-control study comparing lung-cancer patients to controls established the smoking-cancer link a decade before randomized cohort evidence. The 2018 FRQ tested case-control identification and asked students to explain the trade-off: efficient for rare outcomes but vulnerable to recall bias (cases may better remember exposures).

Causation - a true cause-and-effect relationship - can only be established by a well-designed experiment with random assignment of subjects to treatments. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes this as the central principle distinguishing experiments from observational studies: only random assignment guarantees that the groups are balanced on all confounders, allowing causal interpretation of any observed difference. The 2019 FRQ awarded rubric points for students who explicitly cited 'random assignment' (not just 'random sampling') as the key feature supporting causal inference. The Bradford Hill criteria (1965) offer additional standards for causal claims from observational data, but experiments remain the gold standard.

questions.prompt: Variable affecting both x and y.
questions.explanation: A confounding variable is a third variable that affects BOTH the explanatory and response variables, creating a spurious association even if no causal relationship exists. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes confounding as the central threat in observational studies: any observed association could be due to a lurking confounder. The classic example is the positive association between ice cream sales and drowning deaths - both confounded by summer temperature. The 2018 FRQ tested confounding identification in a study of coffee consumption and heart disease: rubric points were awarded for naming a plausible confounder (e.g., smoking, age) and explaining how it could create a spurious association.

Probability is the long-run relative frequency of an event - the proportion of times the event would occur in a very large number of trials. AP Statistics Unit 4 introduces the frequentist interpretation due to Richard von Mises (1928): P(A) is the limit of #A/n as n approaches infinity. Subjective and Bayesian interpretations (Unit 4's Bayes' theorem) treat P(A) as a degree of belief. The 2019 FRQ tested the frequentist interpretation with a simulation question - students who confused 'long-run relative frequency' with 'short-run proportion' lost rubric points. The Law of Large Numbers (Bernoulli 1713) is the theoretical foundation.

P(A or B) - the probability that event A occurs, B occurs, or both - is computed by the general addition rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B). AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes that when A and B are mutually exclusive (cannot both occur), P(A and B) = 0 and the rule simplifies to P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). The 2018 FRQ tested the general addition rule with non-mutually-exclusive events (e.g., students taking AP Statistics OR AP Calculus) - rubric points were awarded for explicitly subtracting the overlap. Forgetting to subtract P(A and B) when events overlap is the single most common error on this topic.

P(A and B) - the probability that BOTH events occur - is computed by the general multiplication rule: P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B|A). AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes that when A and B are independent, P(B|A) = P(B) and the rule simplifies to P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B). The 2019 FRQ tested the multiplication rule with conditional probabilities - rubric points required explicitly using P(B|A), not P(B), when events were dependent. The classic error is assuming independence without justification: e.g., drawing two cards WITHOUT replacement makes the events dependent.

Conditional probability P(A|B) is the probability of A given B has occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B). AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes that P(A|B) is generally NOT equal to P(B|A) - confusing the two is the 'prosecutor's fallacy' (Thompson & Schumann 1987). The 2018 FRQ tested conditional probability with a two-way table - rubric points required correctly identifying which probability was being computed. Conditional probability is the foundation for Bayes' theorem, for tests of independence (chi-square in Unit 8), and for medical-test interpretation (sensitivity and specificity).

Mutually exclusive events (or disjoint events) cannot both occur simultaneously - P(A and B) = 0. AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes a common confusion: mutually exclusive events are NEVER independent (unless one has probability 0). If A and B are mutually exclusive and both have positive probability, then knowing A occurred tells you B did NOT occur, so they cannot be independent. The 2017 FRQ tested this conceptual distinction - rubric points were awarded for identifying that drawing a King and drawing a Queen from a single card are mutually exclusive but NOT independent. Examples: roll a die - 'rolling 1' and 'rolling 2' are mutually exclusive; 'rolling odd' and 'rolling >3' are independent.

questions.prompt: Property where one event does not affect another.
questions.explanation: Two events A and B are INDEPENDENT if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other: P(A|B) = P(A), or equivalently P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B). AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes that independence is a strong assumption, often justified by random sampling, random assignment, or physical setup (e.g., separate coin flips). The 2019 FRQ tested independence assessment with a two-way table - rubric points required checking whether P(A and B) equals P(A) * P(B) numerically, not just claiming independence by intuition. Common errors: confusing independent with mutually exclusive, or assuming independence when sampling without replacement.

When A and B are independent events, the multiplication rule simplifies to P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B). AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes this is a SHORTCUT applicable ONLY when independence is justified - random sampling, random assignment, or independent physical processes. The 2018 FRQ tested the independence multiplication rule on dice and coin problems where independence is obvious. Caution: in real survey data, independence rarely holds, and applying the simplified rule can produce wildly wrong probabilities. Always verify independence before using the shortcut - the 2017 FRQ docked points for assuming independence without justification.

The general multiplication rule P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B|A) applies whether or not A and B are independent. AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes using the general rule when independence is NOT established: e.g., drawing cards without replacement, sampling from a finite population. The 2019 FRQ tested the general rule with sequential events: P(both red marbles) = P(first red) * P(second red | first red). Rubric points required showing the conditional probability P(B|A) explicitly. Confusing the general rule with the simplified independent rule is the second most common error in probability problems on the AP exam.

Bayes' theorem (Reverend Thomas Bayes 1763, published posthumously by Price) states P(A|B) = P(B|A) * P(A) / P(B). AP Statistics Unit 4 introduces Bayes for medical-test problems: given sensitivity (P(test+|disease+)), specificity, and prevalence, compute the positive predictive value P(disease+|test+). The 2018 FRQ tested this: a rare disease (prevalence 1 percent) with 99 percent sensitivity and 95 percent specificity has PPV only about 17 percent - a counterintuitive result that surprises most students. Bayes' theorem is also the foundation of Bayesian statistics, the alternative inferential framework to the frequentist approach used throughout AP Stats.

The Law of Total Probability decomposes P(B) over a partition {A_1, A_2, ..., A_k} of the sample space: P(B) = Sigma P(B|A_i) * P(A_i). AP Statistics Unit 4 uses this law in tree-diagram problems and in deriving Bayes' theorem - the denominator P(B) in Bayes is computed via total probability. The 2019 FRQ tested total probability with a manufacturing scenario: P(defective) = P(defective|machine A) * P(machine A) + P(defective|machine B) * P(machine B). Rubric points required showing the decomposition over all machines, not just one. The law is essential for problems involving multiple sources or stages.

A random variable (RV) is a numerical function defined on the sample space - it assigns a number to each outcome of a random experiment. AP Statistics Unit 4 distinguishes discrete RVs (countable values, e.g., number of heads in 10 flips) from continuous RVs (interval values, e.g., height of a randomly selected adult). The 2018 FRQ tested RV identification: students were asked whether the RV 'number of children in a randomly chosen family' is discrete or continuous - answer: discrete. Random variables are central to inference because parameters like means and proportions are computed as expected values of RVs. Capital letters X, Y, Z denote RVs; lowercase x, y, z denote specific values.

A discrete random variable takes a finite or countably infinite set of values - typically integers or specific values - each with a positive probability. AP Statistics Unit 4 introduces discrete RVs with examples like number of successes in n trials (binomial), trial of first success (geometric), or number of arrivals in a fixed time (Poisson). The probability mass function P(X = x) lists probabilities for each value. The 2019 FRQ tested discrete RV calculations: given a probability distribution table, compute mu = Sigma x * P(x) and sigma = sqrt(Sigma (x - mu)^2 * P(x)). Rubric points required explicit calculation, not calculator output alone.

A continuous random variable takes values in an interval of real numbers - typically R or [a, b] - with probabilities described by a probability density function f(x). AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes that P(X = exact value) = 0 for continuous RVs; probabilities are areas under the density curve over intervals. The Normal distribution is the most important continuous RV in AP Stats. The 2018 FRQ tested continuous RV identification: 'amount of rainfall in a year' is continuous. Computing probabilities for continuous RVs requires either standardizing (z-scores) or using calculator/table functions like normalcdf. The cumulative distribution function F(x) = P(X <= x) is monotonically increasing from 0 to 1.

The expected value (or mean) of a random variable is E(X) = Sigma x * P(X = x) for discrete RVs, or the integral of x*f(x) for continuous RVs. AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes that E(X) is the long-run AVERAGE value of X across many trials, NOT the most likely outcome. The 2019 FRQ tested expected value computation with a probability distribution table: students computed E(X) = Sigma x * P(x) and interpreted it in context - 'the average winnings per game in the long run.' Linearity of expectation E(aX + bY) = aE(X) + bE(Y) is a useful property tested in 2018, and it holds whether or not X and Y are independent.

The standard deviation of a random variable sigma_X = sqrt(Var(X)) measures its spread - the typical deviation of X from its mean. AP Statistics Unit 4 computes Var(X) = Sigma (x - mu)^2 * P(x) for discrete RVs. Key property: Var(aX + b) = a^2 * Var(X), and Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) ONLY when X and Y are independent. The 2018 FRQ tested SD computation and interpretation: a fair die has mu = 3.5 and sigma approximately 1.71, interpreted as 'typical deviation from the mean roll.' Variance addition (only under independence) is the foundation for combining sample distributions, leading directly to the CLT in Unit 5.

questions.prompt: Distribution of successes in n trials.
questions.explanation: The binomial distribution describes the number of successes X in n independent trials, each with probability p of success. AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes that the binomial has 2 parameters (n, p), takes values 0 through n, and has probability mass function P(X = k) = C(n,k) * p^k * (1-p)^(n-k). The 2019 FRQ tested binomial computation: P(at least 3 successes in 10 trials, p = 0.4) - rubric points required either explicit binomial PDF summation or use of 1 - binomcdf with correct bounds. The binomial arises whenever a fixed number of independent yes/no trials are aggregated.

BINS is the acronym for the four conditions required to use the binomial distribution: Binary (each trial is success or failure), Independent (trials don't affect each other), Number of trials n is fixed in advance, Same probability p across trials. AP Statistics Unit 4 expects students to verify all four BINS conditions before applying binomial formulas. The 2018 FRQ docked points for students who applied binomial without verifying independence: sampling 10 students from a class of 30 violates independence because the probabilities change. The 10 percent rule (sample size <= 10 percent of population) restores approximate independence and is the standard AP Stats workaround.

The mean of a binomial distribution is mu_X = n*p. AP Statistics Unit 4 derives this from linearity of expectation: each trial contributes p to the expected count, so E(X) = n*p. The 2019 FRQ tested mu = n*p in context: for n = 20 free-throw attempts at p = 0.75, the expected number of made shots is 15. Rubric points required computation AND contextual interpretation. The mean of the binomial is intuitive and easy to remember; common errors involve mistaking mu = n*p as the median or mode (which are nearby but not equal for small n).

The standard deviation of a binomial distribution is sigma_X = sqrt(n*p*(1-p)). AP Statistics Unit 4 derives this from Var(X) = n*p*(1-p), an immediate consequence of variance additivity for the n independent Bernoulli trials each contributing variance p*(1-p). The 2018 FRQ tested binomial SD: for n = 20, p = 0.75, sigma = sqrt(20*0.75*0.25) = sqrt(3.75) approximately 1.94. Rubric points required correct units AND interpretation. The maximum of sigma occurs at p = 0.5, where p*(1-p) = 0.25 is largest - useful for sample-size planning when p is unknown.

questions.prompt: Distribution counting trials until first success.
questions.explanation: The geometric distribution counts the number of independent trials needed to get the FIRST success, where each trial has probability p of success. AP Statistics Unit 4 contrasts geometric with binomial: binomial fixes n and counts successes; geometric fixes 1 success and counts n. PMF: P(X = k) = (1-p)^(k-1) * p for k = 1, 2, 3, ... Mean: E(X) = 1/p. The 2019 FRQ tested geometric mean: average number of free-throw attempts until the first miss for a 90 percent shooter is 1/0.1 = 10. The geometric has the 'memoryless' property: P(X > j+k | X > j) = P(X > k), unique among discrete distributions.

The Normal distribution N(mu, sigma) is the bell-shaped continuous distribution central to AP Statistics. Formalized by Carl Friedrich Gauss in 1809 to model astronomical measurement errors, it has density f(x) = (1/(sigma*sqrt(2*pi))) * exp(-(x-mu)^2/(2*sigma^2)). AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes the Normal as the foundation for inference via the Central Limit Theorem (CLT). The Normal arises in nature whenever many small random effects combine additively (e.g., human height, measurement error, IQ scores). The 2019 FRQ tested Normal probability computation using the calculator function normalcdf - rubric points required correct boundary inputs AND context interpretation.

The standard Normal distribution N(0, 1) has mean 0 and standard deviation 1. AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes that any Normal random variable X with mean mu and SD sigma can be standardized to Z = (X - mu)/sigma, which follows the standard Normal. This transformation lets students use a single table (or calculator function) to compute all Normal probabilities, regardless of mu and sigma. The 2018 FRQ tested standardization: a Normal IQ distribution with mu = 100, sigma = 15 - 'find P(X > 130)' requires Z = (130 - 100)/15 = 2, then P(Z > 2) = 0.0228 from tables. Rubric points awarded for correct standardization step.

The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7) states that for any Normal distribution, approximately 68 percent of data lie within 1 SD of the mean, 95 percent within 2 SD, and 99.7 percent within 3 SD. AP Statistics Unit 4 uses this rule for fast probability estimates without z-tables. The 2019 FRQ tested Empirical Rule application: a Normal weight distribution with mu = 175, sigma = 20 - what percent are above 215? Since 215 = mu + 2*sigma, approximately 2.5 percent are above (half of 5 percent). Rubric points required correctly applying the symmetry: the rule says 95 percent are WITHIN 2 SD, so 5 percent are outside, split equally between the two tails.

A z-score z = (x - mu)/sigma represents an observation's distance from the mean in units of standard deviation. AP Statistics Unit 4 uses z-scores to (a) compute Normal probabilities via the standard Normal table, (b) compare observations across different Normal distributions, and (c) identify unusual values (|z| > 2 is unusual, |z| > 3 is rare). The 2018 FRQ tested z-score comparison: SAT score 1400 (mu = 1000, sigma = 200) gives z = 2; ACT score 30 (mu = 21, sigma = 4) gives z = 2.25. The ACT score is relatively higher because its z-score is larger. Rubric points required showing both z-scores and explicit comparison.

questions.prompt: Theorem giving sample means as normal.
questions.explanation: The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that the sampling distribution of x-bar (or p-hat) approaches Normal as sample size n grows, regardless of the population distribution. AP Statistics Unit 4 previews the CLT before it is formally introduced in Unit 5. The 2019 FRQ tested the CLT consequence: for any population with mean mu and SD sigma, the sampling distribution of x-bar from n = 50 is approximately N(mu, sigma/sqrt(50)). Rubric points required citing both the mean AND the SD of x-bar. The CLT is the most important theorem in statistics - it justifies using Normal-based inference for means and proportions even when population shape is unknown.

choices: np and n(1-p) both at least 10
questions.explanation: The sampling distribution of p-hat is the probability distribution of the sample proportion p-hat across all possible samples of size n from a population with proportion p. AP Statistics Unit 5 introduces this as the foundation for inference about proportions in Units 6 and 8. By the CLT, p-hat is approximately Normal when the Large Counts condition (np >= 10 and n(1-p) >= 10) holds. The 2019 FRQ tested sampling distribution recognition: for a population proportion p = 0.4 and n = 100, the sampling distribution of p-hat is approximately N(0.4, sqrt(0.4*0.6/100)) = N(0.4, 0.049). Rubric points required citing both the mean AND the SD.

choices: np and n(1-p) both at least 10
questions.explanation: The mean of the sampling distribution of p-hat is exactly p - the population proportion. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes this unbiasedness property: across many samples, the sample proportions average out to the true population proportion. The 2018 FRQ tested this property: 'the long-run average of sample proportions is the population proportion p.' Rubric points required citing p (not p-hat) and explaining the unbiased-estimator concept. This unbiasedness is what makes p-hat a 'fair' estimator of p - it does not systematically overestimate or underestimate. Unbiasedness is one of the two desirable properties of estimators (along with low variability).

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of p-hat is sigma_p-hat = sqrt(p*(1-p)/n), where p is the population proportion and n is the sample size. AP Statistics Unit 5 derives this from the variance of a binomial X = np-hat divided by n^2. The 2019 FRQ tested SD computation: for p = 0.4 and n = 100, sigma_p-hat = sqrt(0.4*0.6/100) = 0.049. Rubric points required showing the formula AND units. As n increases, sigma_p-hat decreases - quadrupling n halves sigma_p-hat. This is why doubling precision (halving margin of error) requires quadrupling sample size, as tested in Unit 6.

questions.prompt: Conditions for p-hat to be normal.
choices: np and n(1-p) both at least 10
questions.correctText: np and n(1-p) both at least 10
questions.explanation: The Large Counts condition requires both np >= 10 AND n(1-p) >= 10 to use the Normal approximation for the sampling distribution of p-hat. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes this as essential before applying any Normal-based inference for proportions in Units 6 and 8. The 2018 FRQ tested this condition: for p = 0.05 and n = 100, np = 5 fails Large Counts, so Normal approximation is invalid. Rubric points required checking BOTH parts of the condition, not just one. When Large Counts fails, exact binomial methods or larger samples are needed. The threshold of 10 is conventional; some texts use 5.

The 10 percent condition (also called the independence condition) requires that the sample size n be no more than 10 percent of the population size N: n <= 0.10*N. AP Statistics Unit 5 introduces this rule to ensure approximate independence when sampling WITHOUT replacement from a finite population. The 2019 FRQ docked points for students who applied Normal inference to a sample of 500 from a population of 1000 - the 10 percent condition fails (50 percent of population), so the simple SD formula overstates variability. Rubric points required citing both Large Counts AND 10 percent conditions before applying Normal approximation in inference for proportions.

The sampling distribution of x-bar is the probability distribution of the sample mean x-bar across all possible samples of size n from a population with mean mu and SD sigma. AP Statistics Unit 5 introduces this as the foundation for inference about means in Units 7 and 9. By the CLT, x-bar is approximately Normal for sufficiently large n, regardless of population shape. The 2018 FRQ tested sampling distribution understanding: for a population with mu = 100 and sigma = 20, samples of n = 50 give x-bar approximately N(100, 20/sqrt(50)) = N(100, 2.83). Rubric points required citing both the mean and the SD - and noting CLT justification.

The mean of the sampling distribution of x-bar is exactly mu - the population mean. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes this unbiasedness: across many samples, the sample means average out to the population mean. The 2019 FRQ tested this property in context: 'the long-run average of sample means is the population mean mu.' Rubric points required citing mu (not x-bar) and explaining unbiasedness. This is why x-bar is the preferred estimator of mu - it has no systematic bias. Unbiasedness combined with the CLT's Normal sampling distribution makes x-bar the foundation of mean inference in Units 7, 9.

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of x-bar is sigma_x-bar = sigma/sqrt(n), where sigma is the population SD and n is the sample size. AP Statistics Unit 5 derives this from Var(X-bar) = sigma^2/n. The 2018 FRQ tested SD computation: for sigma = 20 and n = 50, sigma_x-bar = 20/sqrt(50) = 2.83. Rubric points required showing the formula AND units. As n increases, sigma_x-bar decreases - quadrupling n halves sigma_x-bar. This is the 'square-root law' of sampling: precision improves with sqrt(n), not n. This is why huge samples are needed for tiny precision gains beyond n = 1000.

questions.prompt: Theorem normalizing x-bar for large n.
questions.explanation: The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is the foundational theorem stating that the sampling distribution of x-bar is approximately Normal for sufficiently large n, regardless of the population's distribution. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes the CLT as the single most important theorem in classical statistics - it justifies using Normal inference even when population shape is unknown. The 2019 FRQ tested CLT application: for a strongly right-skewed population, students were asked at what n the CLT assures approximate Normality of x-bar. Rubric points awarded for citing n >= 30 as the conventional threshold. Larger n is needed for stronger skewness; smaller n suffices for already-Normal populations.

choices: x-bar plus or minus t star times SE
questions.explanation: The conventional sample-size rule for the CLT is n >= 30. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes that this threshold is the standard rule for using Normal-based inference about means when population shape is unknown or non-Normal. The 2018 FRQ tested this: a sample of n = 20 from a strongly skewed population is too small for CLT to assure approximate Normality of x-bar - students were expected to recommend caution and a larger sample. Rubric points required citing n >= 30 AND noting that n = 20 may not suffice when skew is severe. For already-Normal populations, even small n suffices. For very skewed populations, n = 30 may not be enough.

questions.prompt: Distribution of difference of two sample proportions.
choices: Sampling distribution of p1-hat minus p2-hat
Sampling distribution of x-bar1 minus x-bar2
questions.correctText: Sampling distribution of p1-hat minus p2-hat
questions.explanation: The sampling distribution of p1-hat minus p2-hat is the distribution of the difference between two sample proportions across all possible pairs of independent samples. AP Statistics Unit 5 introduces this as the foundation for two-proportion inference in Unit 6. By the CLT, the difference is approximately Normal when both Large Counts conditions hold. The 2019 FRQ tested sampling distribution for differences: for p1 = 0.5, p2 = 0.4, n1 = n2 = 100, the difference is approximately Normal with mean 0.1 and SD = sqrt(0.5*0.5/100 + 0.4*0.6/100) = 0.068. Rubric points required showing both means and computed SD.

choices: Sampling distribution of p1-hat minus p2-hat
questions.explanation: The mean of the sampling distribution of p1-hat minus p2-hat is exactly p1 - p2 - the difference of the population proportions. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes this unbiasedness of the difference estimator. The 2018 FRQ tested difference-of-proportions inference: the long-run average difference in sample proportions equals the true population difference. Rubric points required citing the difference (not the individual proportions) AND linking to two-sample inference. Unbiasedness of the difference is what makes p1-hat - p2-hat a valid estimator for p1 - p2 in Unit 6 confidence intervals and hypothesis tests.

choices: Sampling distribution of p1-hat minus p2-hat
questions.explanation: The standard error of the difference p1-hat minus p2-hat is SE = sqrt(p1*(1-p1)/n1 + p2*(1-p2)/n2). This formula assumes independent samples and uses the fact that the variance of a difference of independent random variables is the sum of their variances. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes this 'sum of variances' formula as the foundation for two-sample inference. The 2019 FRQ tested SE computation in a smoking-rate comparison: SE was used to construct a confidence interval for p1 - p2. Rubric points required correct SE formula AND independence justification. For hypothesis tests (Unit 6), a pooled estimate replaces p1, p2 with a single combined p-hat.

questions.prompt: Distribution of difference of sample means.
choices: Sampling distribution of x-bar1 minus x-bar2
Sampling distribution of p1-hat minus p2-hat
questions.correctText: Sampling distribution of x-bar1 minus x-bar2
questions.explanation: The sampling distribution of x-bar1 minus x-bar2 is the distribution of the difference between two sample means across independent samples. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes this as the foundation for two-sample t inference in Unit 7. By the CLT, the difference is approximately Normal when each sample's Normality condition is met. The 2018 FRQ tested two-sample sampling distribution: for mu1 = 75, mu2 = 70, sigma1 = sigma2 = 10, n1 = n2 = 25, the difference x-bar1 - x-bar2 is approximately Normal with mean 5 and SD = sqrt(100/25 + 100/25) = sqrt(8) = 2.83. Rubric points awarded for showing the variance addition.

questions.prompt: Condition needed for difference standard error.
choices: Sampling distribution of p1-hat minus p2-hat
questions.explanation: Independent samples - the samples are drawn separately and one does not affect the other - is the key condition for variance addition: Var(x-bar1 - x-bar2) = Var(x-bar1) + Var(x-bar2). AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes that independence is verified by random assignment (in experiments) or by separate random samples (in observational studies). The 2019 FRQ docked points for students who applied independent-samples inference to a matched-pairs design - paired data are NOT independent and require paired-sample procedures instead (Unit 7). Independence is the critical assumption distinguishing two-sample procedures from paired procedures.

questions.prompt: Property of estimator centered at true value.
questions.explanation: An unbiased estimator is one whose sampling distribution has mean equal to the parameter it estimates: E(estimator) = parameter. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes that x-bar is unbiased for mu, p-hat is unbiased for p, and s^2 (with n-1 divisor) is unbiased for sigma^2. The 2018 FRQ tested unbiasedness: students were asked which of two estimators (sample range vs. sample SD) is unbiased for population SD - answer: neither exactly, but sample SD is approximately unbiased. Rubric points required defining unbiasedness in terms of long-run average matching the parameter. Unbiasedness is one of two desirable estimator properties; low variability is the other.

questions.prompt: Spread of estimator over many samples.
choices: x-bar1 minus x-bar2 plus minus t star SE
questions.explanation: Variability is the spread of an estimator's sampling distribution - typically measured by its standard error (SE). AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes that low variability means the estimator is consistent: across many samples, estimates cluster tightly around the parameter. The 2019 FRQ tested variability assessment in choosing between estimators: a lower-variability estimator is more precise, all else equal. Rubric points required citing both unbiasedness AND low variability as criteria for choosing an estimator. Variability decreases with sample size n: typically SE proportional to 1/sqrt(n), so quadrupling n halves SE.

questions.prompt: Effect of larger sample size on variability.
