questions.explanation: Larger sample size n DECREASES the variability of an estimator - typically by a factor of 1/sqrt(n). AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes this 'square-root law' as the foundation for sample-size planning in Unit 6. The 2018 FRQ tested this property: doubling n from 100 to 200 reduces SE by factor sqrt(2)/2 approximately 0.707, NOT by half. Rubric points required citing the sqrt(n) relationship. This is why precision improvements get progressively more expensive: going from n=100 to n=400 halves SE; going from n=400 to n=1600 halves it again. The trade-off between sample size and precision is central to study planning.

questions.prompt: Effect on variability of larger population.
questions.explanation: Population size has NO effect on the variability of x-bar or p-hat as long as the sample size n is no more than 10 percent of the population. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes this counterintuitive fact: a poll of 1000 voters has the same precision in the U.S. (population 330 million) as in New York (population 20 million). The 2019 FRQ tested this: students were asked whether sample size or population size matters for SE - answer: sample size, not population size. Rubric points required citing the 10 percent rule and noting that even huge populations don't inflate SE. This finding was surprising to early pollsters (Gallup 1936) and remains so to students.

An efficient estimator is one with the smallest possible variance among unbiased estimators - achieving the Cramer-Rao lower bound. AP Statistics Unit 5 introduces this as a refinement of unbiasedness: among multiple unbiased estimators, the efficient one has the lowest SE and thus the highest precision. The 2018 FRQ tested efficiency conceptually: x-bar is more efficient than the sample median as an estimator of mu when the population is Normal. Rubric points required citing efficiency as a property of unbiased estimators distinguished by minimum variance. In AP Stats, efficient estimators (x-bar, p-hat) are the default; alternatives (median, midrange) are less efficient.

Simulation is the use of random number generators to model sampling distributions empirically - drawing many samples computationally to approximate the theoretical sampling distribution. AP Statistics Unit 5 introduces simulation as a way to (a) verify CLT applies, (b) approximate hard-to-compute probabilities, and (c) build intuition. The 2019 FRQ tested simulation design: students were asked to describe a simulation for the sampling distribution of p-hat. Rubric points required specifying the random mechanism, sample size, number of trials, and statistic computed each time. Simulation is now standard in AP Stats and increasingly common in real research.

A random number generator (RNG) is the mechanism that produces pseudo-random values for simulations. AP Statistics Unit 5 expects students to describe RNGs explicitly: e.g., 'use the calculator's randInt(1, 100) function' or 'use a random number table starting at row 5.' The 2018 FRQ docked points for students who said 'randomly select' without specifying the mechanism. Rubric points required either a specific calculator command, table reference, or computer pseudo-random algorithm. Common RNG sources include calculator built-in functions, statistical software (R, Python), or printed random number tables.

A histogram of statistics computed across many simulated samples visualizes the empirical sampling distribution - the central focus of simulation-based inference. AP Statistics Unit 5 expects students to construct and interpret such histograms: shape, center, and spread approximate the theoretical sampling distribution. The 2019 FRQ tested simulation interpretation: a histogram of 1000 sample proportions from samples of n = 50 should center near the population proportion p and have spread approximately sqrt(p*(1-p)/50). Rubric points required matching the empirical center and spread to theoretical values. This visualization makes the CLT tangible to students.

Empirical proportion is the relative frequency of an event in a simulation - the count of trials where the event occurred divided by the total trials. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes that empirical proportions approximate theoretical probabilities by the Law of Large Numbers: as trials -> infinity, empirical proportion -> theoretical probability. The 2018 FRQ tested empirical-probability computation: out of 1000 simulated trials, 387 resulted in 'success,' so empirical proportion = 0.387. Rubric points required dividing by total trials AND noting the approximation to true probability. Empirical proportions are the foundation for randomization-based inference (an alternative to traditional t and z tests).

A bell-shaped histogram of simulated sample statistics confirms the CLT's prediction of approximately Normal sampling distributions. AP Statistics Unit 5 expects students to recognize bell-shape as evidence the CLT applies and Normal inference is justified. The 2019 FRQ tested this connection: a histogram of x-bar from many samples of n = 50 should look approximately Normal even if the population is skewed. Rubric points awarded for citing CLT to explain the bell shape. Conversely, a skewed histogram of sample statistics suggests either too small n or a deeper issue with the simulation design. Visual inspection is the standard simulation check.

questions.prompt: Interval estimating parameter with given confidence.
choices: Sampling distribution of p1-hat minus p2-hat
p-hat plus or minus z star times SE
questions.explanation: A confidence interval (CI) is an interval estimate of a population parameter with a stated level of confidence - typically 90, 95, or 99 percent. AP Statistics Unit 6 introduces the CI as the standard alternative to a point estimate, communicating both the value and the uncertainty. The CI for a proportion has the form p-hat plus or minus z*-times-SE. The 2019 FRQ tested CI construction: for p-hat = 0.6, n = 100, the 95 percent CI is 0.6 plus or minus 1.96*sqrt(0.6*0.4/100) = 0.6 plus or minus 0.096 = (0.504, 0.696). Rubric points required identifying the components AND interpreting in context: 'we are 95 percent confident the population proportion is between 0.504 and 0.696.'

questions.prompt: Critical value for 95 percent normal CI.
questions.explanation: z* = 1.96 is the critical value for a 95 percent Normal-based confidence interval - it captures the central 95 percent of the standard Normal distribution between z = -1.96 and z = +1.96. AP Statistics Unit 6 expects students to know the standard z* values: 1.645 for 90 percent, 1.96 for 95 percent, 2.576 for 99 percent. The 2018 FRQ tested CI construction with z* = 1.96 in the 95 percent case. Rubric points required citing 1.96 explicitly (not 2) and showing the calculation. Rounding 1.96 to 2 is a common error that produces noticeably wider intervals and loses rubric points.

choices: p-hat plus or minus z star times SE
questions.correctText: p-hat plus or minus z star times SE
questions.explanation: The one-proportion CI formula is p-hat plus or minus z*-times-sqrt(p-hat*(1-p-hat)/n). AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that this uses p-hat (not p) in the SE because the population p is unknown - we plug in the best estimate. The 2019 FRQ tested CI computation: for p-hat = 0.6, n = 100, 95 percent CI = (0.504, 0.696). Rubric points required (a) checking Large Counts and 10 percent conditions, (b) computing SE correctly with p-hat, and (c) interpreting in context. Conditions must be verified BEFORE computing - skipping condition verification costs at least one rubric point.

questions.prompt: Width of CI doubles when this happens.
choices: p-hat plus or minus z star times SE
questions.explanation: The width of a confidence interval doubles when the standard error doubles - all else equal. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes the CI half-width (margin of error) ME = z*-times-SE, so widening SE proportionally widens the CI. The 2018 FRQ tested CI width: if a survey doubles its variability (e.g., by sampling a more heterogeneous group), the CI doubles in width. Rubric points required citing both the SE relationship AND a contextual implication: less precision means less informative CI. Conversely, to NARROW a CI by half, SE must be halved - typically requiring 4 times the sample size.

questions.prompt: To halve margin of error you must do this.
choices: p-hat plus or minus z star times SE
questions.explanation: To halve the margin of error of a confidence interval, sample size must be QUADRUPLED. AP Statistics Unit 6 derives this from ME proportional to 1/sqrt(n): halving ME requires multiplying n by 4. The 2019 FRQ tested sample-size planning: a current poll has ME = 4 percent at n = 600; to achieve ME = 2 percent, n must be 2400. Rubric points required showing the quadrupling reasoning AND arriving at the correct n. This 'square-root law' of precision is why doubling poll accuracy is so expensive - and why national polls are typically capped at n = 1000-2000, beyond which further precision gains are minimal.

questions.prompt: Test that p equals a given value.
questions.explanation: The one-proportion z-test tests the null hypothesis H0: p = p0 against an alternative (one-sided or two-sided) using the test statistic z = (p-hat - p0)/sqrt(p0*(1-p0)/n). AP Statistics Unit 6 introduces this as the standard test for a single proportion. The 2018 FRQ tested the one-proportion z-test in a manufacturing context: H0: p = 0.05 (defect rate) vs Ha: p > 0.05. Rubric points required (a) stating hypotheses with p (not p-hat), (b) checking conditions, (c) computing the test statistic, and (d) interpreting the P-value in context. The standard error in the test uses p0 (not p-hat) because we assume H0 is true.

The null hypothesis in a one-proportion test has the form H0: p = p0, where p0 is the hypothesized value (e.g., 0.5, 0.05, 0.50). AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that hypotheses are statements about parameters (p) NOT sample statistics (p-hat) - a common error. The alternative hypothesis is Ha: p ≠ p0 (two-sided), p > p0, or p < p0 (one-sided). The 2019 FRQ docked points for students who wrote H0: p-hat = 0.5 - hypotheses are about populations, not samples. Rubric points required correct parameter notation AND a contextual sentence: 'H0: the true defect rate equals 5 percent.'

The test statistic for a one-proportion z-test is z = (p-hat - p0)/sqrt(p0*(1-p0)/n) - the number of standard errors p-hat lies from p0 under H0. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes using p0 (not p-hat) in the SE because the test assumes H0 is true. The 2018 FRQ tested z computation: for p-hat = 0.07, n = 500, p0 = 0.05, z = (0.07 - 0.05)/sqrt(0.05*0.95/500) = 0.02/0.00975 = 2.05. Rubric points required showing the formula AND using p0 (not p-hat) in the denominator. Confusing p0 with p-hat in SE is the most common error in proportion tests.

questions.prompt: Probability of observing test statistic given null.
questions.explanation: The P-value is the probability of observing a test statistic as extreme as (or more extreme than) the one observed, assuming H0 is true. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes the P-value as a measure of evidence against H0: small P-values indicate strong evidence. For a one-sided z-test, P-value = P(Z > z) or P(Z < z); for two-sided, P-value = 2 * P(Z > |z|). The 2019 FRQ tested P-value computation and interpretation: for z = 2.05, two-sided P-value = 2 * 0.0202 = 0.0404. Rubric points required citing 'probability of observing data this extreme assuming H0 true.' Calling the P-value 'the probability H0 is true' is a common error that loses points.

questions.prompt: Conclusion when P less than alpha.
questions.explanation: When the P-value is less than alpha (the significance level), the conclusion is to REJECT the null hypothesis - the evidence is strong enough to support the alternative. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that 'reject' does not mean 'prove false' - it means the evidence is unlikely under H0. The 2018 FRQ tested this conclusion in context: P-value = 0.02 < alpha = 0.05, so reject H0 and conclude there is convincing evidence that the defect rate exceeds 5 percent. Rubric points required full contextual interpretation, not just 'reject H0.' The decision is binary (reject or fail to reject), but the language must be linked to the research question.

The two-proportion z-test compares two population proportions p1 and p2 using independent samples. AP Statistics Unit 6 introduces this as the standard test for comparing two groups (e.g., treatment vs control) in surveys and experiments. The test statistic uses a POOLED estimate p-hat = (x1 + x2)/(n1 + n2), which assumes H0: p1 = p2. The 2019 FRQ tested two-proportion comparison in a smoking-rate study: H0: p1 = p2 vs Ha: p1 ≠ p2. Rubric points required (a) hypotheses, (b) condition check on both samples, (c) pooled p-hat, (d) z statistic, (e) P-value, and (f) contextual conclusion. Two-proportion tests dominate election-poll comparisons.

The null hypothesis in a two-proportion test has the form H0: p1 = p2, equivalent to H0: p1 - p2 = 0. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that hypotheses are about the population proportions, not sample proportions. The alternative is Ha: p1 ≠ p2 (two-sided), p1 > p2, or p1 < p2 (one-sided), depending on the research question. The 2018 FRQ docked points for students who wrote H0: p-hat1 = p-hat2 (using sample statistics) instead of H0: p1 = p2 (using parameters). Rubric points required correct parameter notation AND a contextual sentence: 'H0: the smoking rates in the two populations are equal.'

The pooled p-hat is the combined sample proportion when H0: p1 = p2 is assumed: p-hat = (x1 + x2)/(n1 + n2). AP Statistics Unit 6 uses pooled p-hat in the test statistic's SE: SE = sqrt(p-hat*(1-p-hat)*(1/n1 + 1/n2)). The 2019 FRQ tested pooled p-hat computation: x1 = 24, n1 = 100, x2 = 18, n2 = 100, so pooled p-hat = 42/200 = 0.21. Rubric points required correct pooling AND using it in SE. Note: confidence intervals do NOT use pooled p-hat - they use the separate p-hats - because CIs don't assume the proportions are equal.

choices: p-hat1 minus p-hat2 plus minus z star SE
questions.correctText: p-hat1 minus p-hat2 plus minus z star SE
questions.explanation: The two-proportion CI formula is (p-hat1 - p-hat2) plus or minus z*-times-sqrt(p-hat1*(1-p-hat1)/n1 + p-hat2*(1-p-hat2)/n2). AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that this CI uses separate p-hats (no pooling) because we are NOT assuming p1 = p2 - we are estimating the difference. The 2018 FRQ tested CI construction: for p-hat1 = 0.4, p-hat2 = 0.3, n1 = n2 = 100, 95 percent CI = 0.1 plus or minus 1.96*sqrt(0.4*0.6/100 + 0.3*0.7/100) = (0.0117, 0.1883). Rubric points required separate SE terms AND contextual interpretation: 'we are 95 percent confident the difference is between 1.17 and 18.83 percentage points.'

choices: p-hat1 minus p-hat2 plus minus z star SE
questions.explanation: The conditions for two-proportion inference are Independence (independent samples, 10 percent rule for each) AND Large Counts (n1*p1, n1*(1-p1), n2*p2, n2*(1-p2) all >= 10). AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes checking BOTH conditions BEFORE applying any procedures. The 2019 FRQ docked points for students who skipped condition verification on a two-proportion test. Rubric points required explicit checking of independence AND all four large-count expressions. For tests, use pooled p-hat in Large Counts; for CIs, use separate p-hats. Failure to verify conditions is the second most common error on two-proportion problems on the AP exam.

A Type I error is rejecting H0 when H0 is true - a 'false positive.' AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that the probability of Type I error is the significance level alpha, chosen in advance. The 2019 FRQ tested Type I error interpretation: in a drug-efficacy trial, Type I error means concluding the drug works when it actually doesn't. Rubric points required defining Type I in terms of rejecting true H0 AND giving a contextual example. The cost of Type I error depends on context: in criminal trials it means convicting an innocent person; in drug trials it means approving an ineffective drug.

A Type II error is failing to reject H0 when H0 is false - a 'false negative.' AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that the probability of Type II error is beta, and 1 - beta is power (the probability of correctly rejecting false H0). The 2018 FRQ tested Type II error interpretation: in a drug-efficacy trial, Type II error means concluding the drug doesn't work when it actually does. Rubric points required defining Type II in terms of failing to reject false H0 AND giving a contextual example. Type II error is generally harder to control than Type I because beta depends on the unknown true parameter value.

questions.prompt: Probability of avoiding Type II error.
questions.explanation: Power is the probability of correctly rejecting a false H0 - the probability of detecting a real effect. AP Statistics Unit 6 defines power = 1 - beta, where beta is the probability of Type II error. Power depends on (a) sample size (larger n -> more power), (b) effect size (larger true difference -> more power), (c) variability (more variability -> less power), and (d) alpha (larger alpha -> more power). The 2019 FRQ tested power in context: a study with power 0.80 has an 80 percent chance of detecting an effect if one exists. Rubric points required defining power in terms of detecting real effects, not just '1 - beta.'

choices: x-bar plus or minus t star times SE
questions.explanation: Increasing sample size n is the most reliable way to INCREASE the power of a test. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes this because larger n reduces SE, which makes test statistics more extreme for the same true effect size, leading to smaller P-values and more rejections of false H0. The 2018 FRQ tested power factors: among (a) larger alpha, (b) larger effect size, (c) larger n, (d) less variability - all increase power. Rubric points required identifying multiple factors AND ranking them by typical magnitude of effect: sample size is usually the biggest practical lever. Researchers planning studies use power calculations to choose n that achieves target power (usually 0.80).

Decreasing alpha (reducing Type I error rate) INCREASES the probability of Type II error - a fundamental trade-off in hypothesis testing. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that alpha and beta are inversely related when n is fixed: requiring stronger evidence to reject H0 makes it harder to detect real effects. The 2019 FRQ tested this trade-off: changing alpha from 0.05 to 0.01 reduces Type I error but reduces power. Rubric points required citing the trade-off AND describing it as inherent to fixed-n testing. The only way to reduce BOTH error rates simultaneously is to increase sample size - the central lesson of power analysis.

The significance level alpha is the threshold for rejecting H0 - typically 0.10, 0.05, or 0.01. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes choosing alpha BEFORE seeing the data, to maintain the validity of the Type I error rate. The 2018 FRQ tested alpha selection in context: a critical medical decision (drug efficacy) might use alpha = 0.01; a routine quality-control test might use alpha = 0.05 or 0.10. Rubric points required citing context-appropriate alpha and explaining the trade-off: smaller alpha gives stronger evidence threshold but higher beta. The convention alpha = 0.05 has been criticized as arbitrary since R.A. Fisher proposed it casually in 1925, but remains standard.

questions.prompt: P-value less than alpha leads to this.
questions.explanation: When the P-value is less than alpha, the conclusion is to REJECT H0 - the evidence is strong enough to support the alternative. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that rejection conclusions must be stated in context, not just 'reject H0.' The 2019 FRQ tested the full conclusion: P-value = 0.02 < alpha = 0.05, so 'reject H0 and conclude there is convincing evidence that the population proportion exceeds the hypothesized value.' Rubric points required contextual language AND linking back to the research question. The decision is binary, but the interpretation must be tied to the substantive claim being tested.

questions.prompt: Statistical significance does not imply this.
questions.explanation: Statistical significance does NOT imply PRACTICAL importance. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that with large samples, even tiny effects can be statistically significant - but may be too small to matter in practice. The 2018 FRQ tested this distinction: a study with n = 10,000 found a 0.1 percentage-point difference in defect rates is statistically significant (P < 0.001) but practically meaningless. Rubric points required explaining the difference between statistical significance and practical importance. The reverse can also hold: a large effect may fail to achieve significance with small n. Effect sizes (e.g., Cohen's d, mean differences) are needed alongside P-values.

Lowering alpha (e.g., from 0.05 to 0.01) makes it HARDER to reject H0 - because the P-value must be smaller to achieve significance. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes this trade-off: stricter alpha gives stronger evidence threshold but reduces power. The 2019 FRQ tested alpha selection: a critical medical decision might use alpha = 0.01, requiring stronger evidence; the cost is higher Type II error. Rubric points required citing the trade-off explicitly: alpha down -> Type I error down but Type II error up. The choice of alpha should balance the costs of the two error types in context.

questions.prompt: Effect size combined with sample size.
questions.explanation: Power of a test is the probability of correctly rejecting a false H0 - jointly determined by sample size, effect size, variability, and alpha. AP Statistics Unit 6 emphasizes that power = 1 - beta = probability of detecting a real effect. The 2018 FRQ tested power factors quantitatively: increasing n from 100 to 400 typically doubles power; reducing variability by half also doubles power; a larger effect size dramatically increases power. Rubric points required listing AT LEAST 3 power factors AND explaining the direction of each. Power calculations - now standard in study planning - typically aim for power = 0.80 (the 'reasonable detection' threshold).

choices: x-bar plus or minus t star times SE
questions.explanation: The Student's t distribution is used for inference about a population mean when the population SD sigma is unknown - which is essentially always in practice. AP Statistics Unit 7 introduces t, developed by William Sealy Gosset in 1908 while working as a chemist at Guinness Brewery (publishing under the pseudonym 'Student' for proprietary reasons). The t distribution looks like Normal but has heavier tails to account for the extra uncertainty from estimating sigma with s. The 2019 FRQ tested t recognition: when sigma is unknown, students must use t (not z) procedures - rubric points docked for using z when sigma is estimated.

questions.prompt: Degrees of freedom for one-sample t.
questions.explanation: Degrees of freedom for a one-sample t procedure are n - 1. AP Statistics Unit 7 derives this from the fact that estimating mu with x-bar consumes one degree of freedom (the Bessel correction). The 2018 FRQ tested df identification: for a sample of n = 25, df = 24. Rubric points required correctly identifying df AND using it to find t* or the P-value. Larger df means the t distribution approaches Normal (as expected from CLT). For n = 30, df = 29 and t* is close to 2.045 (vs z* = 1.96 for 95 percent CI). The t* values are slightly larger than corresponding z*, reflecting the extra uncertainty.

choices: x-bar plus or minus t star times SE
questions.explanation: The one-sample t-test statistic is t = (x-bar - mu0)/(s/sqrt(n)), where s is the sample SD. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes the parallel to z = (x-bar - mu0)/(sigma/sqrt(n)): same form, but s replaces sigma. The 2019 FRQ tested t computation: for x-bar = 75, s = 10, n = 25, mu0 = 70, t = (75 - 70)/(10/5) = 2.5. Rubric points required showing the formula AND using s (not sigma) in the denominator. The t statistic, like z, measures how many standard errors x-bar lies from mu0 under H0 - but with extra uncertainty from estimating sigma.

choices: x-bar plus or minus t star times SE
questions.correctText: x-bar plus or minus t star times SE
questions.explanation: The one-sample t confidence interval is x-bar plus or minus t*-times-(s/sqrt(n)), where t* is the critical value with df = n - 1. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that this is the t analog of the z CI for proportions. The 2018 FRQ tested CI construction: for x-bar = 75, s = 10, n = 25, df = 24, t* for 95 percent CI is 2.064, so CI = 75 plus or minus 2.064*(10/5) = 75 plus or minus 4.13 = (70.87, 79.13). Rubric points required correct t* AND contextual interpretation: 'we are 95 percent confident the population mean is between 70.87 and 79.13.' Note t* > z* due to extra uncertainty.

choices: x-bar plus or minus t star times SE
questions.explanation: Conditions for one-sample t inference are Random (random sample), Normal (population approximately Normal OR n >= 30 by CLT), and Independent (independent observations, 10 percent rule). AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes verifying all three before applying t procedures. The 2019 FRQ docked points for skipping Normality check on a small sample. For n < 15, the data should look very Normal (use boxplot or Normal probability plot); for 15 <= n < 30, mild skew is acceptable; for n >= 30, CLT handles most population shapes. Rubric points required citing the appropriate Normality criterion for the given n.

The two-sample t-test compares two population means mu1 and mu2 using independent samples. AP Statistics Unit 7 introduces this as the standard test for comparing groups when sigma1 and sigma2 are unknown - the typical real-world case. The 2018 FRQ tested two-sample comparison in an exercise-and-weight-loss study: H0: mu1 = mu2 vs Ha: mu1 ≠ mu2. Rubric points required (a) hypotheses, (b) condition check on both samples, (c) Welch-Satterthwaite df, (d) t statistic, (e) P-value, and (f) contextual conclusion. Unlike z procedures, two-sample t does NOT pool variances by default in modern practice.

choices: x-bar1 minus x-bar2 plus minus t star SE
questions.explanation: Welch-Satterthwaite approximation gives the degrees of freedom for an unpooled two-sample t-test, a complex formula df = (s1^2/n1 + s2^2/n2)^2 / [(s1^2/n1)^2/(n1-1) + (s2^2/n2)^2/(n2-1)]. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes using technology (calculator's 2-SampTTest) to compute Welch df. The 2019 FRQ tested df identification: students were expected to read df from calculator output, not compute by hand. Rubric points required citing the df from calculator. The Welch df is typically NOT an integer and is smaller than the simpler min(n1-1, n2-1). For equal sample sizes and similar SDs, Welch df approaches n1 + n2 - 2.

choices: x-bar1 minus x-bar2 plus minus t star SE
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis in a two-sample t-test has the form H0: mu1 = mu2, equivalent to H0: mu1 - mu2 = 0. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that hypotheses are about population means, not sample means. The alternative is Ha: mu1 ≠ mu2 (two-sided), mu1 > mu2, or mu1 < mu2. The 2018 FRQ docked points for students who wrote H0: x-bar1 = x-bar2 (sample) instead of H0: mu1 = mu2 (population). Rubric points required correct parameter notation AND a contextual sentence: 'H0: the mean weight loss in the exercise and control groups are equal.' Match the alternative to the research question's directionality.

choices: x-bar1 minus x-bar2 plus minus t star SE
questions.correctText: x-bar1 minus x-bar2 plus minus t star SE
questions.explanation: The two-sample t CI formula is (x-bar1 - x-bar2) plus or minus t*-times-sqrt(s1^2/n1 + s2^2/n2), where t* is the critical value with Welch df. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that this CI uses separate variances (no pooling) - reflecting the variability of each sample independently. The 2019 FRQ tested two-sample CI: for x-bar1 = 80, x-bar2 = 75, s1 = 10, s2 = 12, n1 = n2 = 30, 95 percent CI for the difference = (5 - margin, 5 + margin). Rubric points required separate SE terms AND contextual interpretation: 'we are 95 percent confident the difference in means is between [low] and [high].'

choices: x-bar1 minus x-bar2 plus minus t star SE
questions.explanation: Conditions for two-sample t inference are Random (independent random samples or random assignment), Normal (each population approximately Normal OR each n >= 30), and Independent (independent samples plus 10 percent rule for each). AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes verifying all three. The 2018 FRQ docked points for skipping condition checks on small samples. Equal variance assumption is NOT required in modern unpooled t-tests; Welch handles unequal variances. Rubric points required explicit verification of Normality for each sample separately - one Normal and one skewed is NOT acceptable for unpooled t with small samples.

questions.prompt: Design where each pair gets both treatments.
questions.explanation: A matched-pairs design pairs each subject (or experimental unit) with another similar one, then randomly assigns treatments within each pair - OR uses each subject as their own control with two measurements. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that matched pairs DESIGN produces matched-pairs DATA, which must be analyzed by computing differences within pairs. The 2019 FRQ tested matched-pairs identification: a study of before-and-after blood pressure for the same patients is matched pairs, NOT two-sample. Rubric points required correctly identifying the design AND using paired t procedures, not two-sample t.

questions.prompt: Statistic used in matched pairs test.
questions.explanation: In a matched-pairs analysis, the statistic of interest is the MEAN DIFFERENCE d-bar = mean of (X1 - X2) across pairs. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that pairing reduces variability by eliminating between-subject variation - the same subject's measurements share confounders. The 2018 FRQ tested d-bar computation: for paired before/after weight data with mean difference of -2.5 lbs, students computed d-bar = -2.5. Rubric points required computing the mean of the differences (not the difference of the means - which equals d-bar in symmetric designs but loses pairing benefit when used). Always compute differences first, then their mean.

The paired t-test analyzes matched-pairs data by computing the differences d_i = X1_i - X2_i within each pair, then applying a one-sample t-test to the differences with H0: mu_d = 0. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes the parallel: paired t-test = one-sample t-test on differences. The 2019 FRQ tested paired t computation: t = (d-bar - 0)/(s_d/sqrt(n)) with df = n - 1 (number of PAIRS minus 1). Rubric points required computing the test statistic correctly AND citing df based on number of pairs. Confusing paired and two-sample procedures is the most common error in Unit 7.

Pairing improves precision by REDUCING variability - because pair-level differences eliminate between-subject confounders that would otherwise inflate noise. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that paired designs have smaller SE than equivalent two-sample designs when within-pair correlation is positive. The 2018 FRQ tested this in a drug-efficacy study comparing before/after blood pressure: pairing eliminated patient-to-patient variability in baseline blood pressure, isolating the drug's effect. Rubric points required citing variance reduction as the reason pairing improves power. When within-pair correlation is zero or negative, pairing may not help - but in practice it usually does.

questions.prompt: Degrees of freedom in paired t.
questions.explanation: Degrees of freedom for a paired t-test is n - 1, where n is the number of PAIRS (not the number of measurements). AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes this distinction: for 30 paired measurements (60 individual values), df = 29, NOT 58. The 2019 FRQ tested df identification: students were expected to count pairs and subtract 1. Rubric points required citing pairs - not total measurements. This is the key formula difference between paired and two-sample t: paired uses n - 1 (pairs), while two-sample uses Welch df (often n1 + n2 - 2 ish). Larger df means narrower CIs, so pairing benefits precision both through reduced variability AND additional df flexibility.

questions.prompt: Property of t-test working despite mild non-normality.
questions.explanation: Robustness is the property of a statistical procedure to work approximately correctly even when its assumptions are mildly violated. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that t procedures are robust to mild departures from Normality - especially for large samples. The 2018 FRQ tested robustness assessment: a moderately skewed sample of n = 50 should still be analyzable by t procedures because of CLT (n >= 30) and the procedure's robustness. Rubric points required citing both n size AND the robustness property. The t-test is NOT robust to (a) outliers in small samples, (b) clear non-randomness (e.g., convenience samples), or (c) clear violations of independence.

Large n makes t-tests robust to non-Normality because the Central Limit Theorem assures approximately Normal sampling distributions of x-bar regardless of population shape. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes the n >= 30 threshold as the standard rule of thumb for t robustness. The 2019 FRQ tested this: a sample of n = 50 from a moderately skewed population is acceptable for t inference; a sample of n = 10 from the same population is NOT. Rubric points required citing the conventional n >= 30 rule AND noting that severe skewness or outliers may require larger n or transformations. CLT does NOT fix non-randomness or independence violations.

questions.prompt: Way to check normality of data.
questions.explanation: Normality of sample data is checked using histograms, dotplots, boxplots, and Normal probability plots (Q-Q plots). AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that for n < 30, students must justify the Normality assumption with a graphical check OR an appeal to a Normally-distributed population (rare). The 2018 FRQ tested Normality assessment: students were given a small-sample boxplot showing clear right skew and asked whether t procedures applied - answer: NO, t is not robust to severe skew with small n. Rubric points required examining the graph AND making a conclusion about Normality. Normal probability plots are the gold standard but rarely used at the AP level.

questions.prompt: Sign of strong departure from normality.
questions.explanation: Strong departures from Normality - heavy skewness, severe outliers, or multimodal distributions - violate t-test assumptions and require non-parametric alternatives or transformations. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that t is NOT robust to severe non-Normality in small samples. The 2019 FRQ tested non-Normality identification: a sample with a clear outlier and small n required either removing the outlier (with justification), transforming the data (log, sqrt), or using a non-parametric procedure (rank-based). Rubric points required citing the violation AND proposing a remedy. Always investigate, never delete, outliers without justification.

Non-parametric tests (sign test, Wilcoxon signed-rank, Wilcoxon rank-sum) provide alternatives to t when Normality is violated and samples are small. AP Statistics Unit 7 mentions these as alternatives, though they are not extensively tested on the AP exam. The 2018 FRQ asked students to recommend a non-parametric test when n = 8 with heavy skewness - any reasonable alternative (sign test, transformation, larger sample) earned credit. Rubric points required citing the limitation of t AND proposing a specific remedy. Modern non-parametric methods include bootstrap-based confidence intervals, which are increasingly common in practice but beyond AP Stats.

choices: t star with df n minus 1
questions.explanation: The point estimate of the population mean mu is the sample mean x-bar. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that x-bar is the best single estimate of mu - unbiased and (under Normal populations) the most efficient. The 2019 FRQ tested point-estimate identification: for a sample with x-bar = 75.2, the point estimate of mu is 75.2. Rubric points required citing x-bar (not the median or any robust estimator). Combined with the SE = s/sqrt(n), the point estimate forms the center of a confidence interval. Point estimates alone are insufficient for inference - they need a CI or hypothesis test to convey uncertainty.

choices: t star with df n minus 1
questions.explanation: A confidence interval provides an INTERVAL estimate of the population mean, combining the point estimate x-bar with a margin of error: x-bar plus or minus t*-times-(s/sqrt(n)). AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that CIs are the standard interval estimate for means. The 2018 FRQ tested CI interpretation: 'we are 95 percent confident the true mean lies between [low] and [high].' Rubric points required (a) the interval, (b) the confidence level, and (c) the parameter being estimated (population mean) - in context. CIs communicate both the estimate AND the uncertainty in one statement.

choices: t star with df n minus 1
questions.explanation: The standard error of x-bar for one sample is SE = s/sqrt(n), where s is the sample SD and n is the sample size. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that SE measures the typical variability of x-bar across samples - smaller SE means more precise estimation. The 2019 FRQ tested SE computation: for s = 10, n = 25, SE = 2. Rubric points required showing the formula AND citing units. SE differs from SD: SD describes variability of individual observations; SE describes variability of the sample mean. SE shrinks with sqrt(n), the square-root law of sampling precision.

questions.prompt: Critical value for 95 percent CI for mean.
choices: t star with df n minus 1
questions.correctText: t star with df n minus 1
questions.explanation: The critical value t* for a 95 percent CI for the mean with df = n - 1 depends on n. AP Statistics Unit 7 expects students to use the t-table or calculator function invT to find t*. The 2018 FRQ tested t* identification: for n = 30, df = 29, 95 percent CI uses t* = 2.045. Rubric points required correctly looking up t* (not z*). Note t* values are slightly LARGER than z* = 1.96 for any finite df, reflecting the extra uncertainty from estimating sigma. As df increases, t* approaches z* = 1.96. For df = 100, t* = 1.984 - already very close to z*.

questions.prompt: Effect of larger sample on CI width.
questions.explanation: A larger sample size produces a NARROWER confidence interval - by the sqrt(n) law of SE. AP Statistics Unit 7 emphasizes that quadrupling n halves the margin of error; doubling n shrinks it by factor sqrt(2). The 2019 FRQ tested sample-size planning for CI width: to halve the margin of error, n must quadruple. Rubric points required citing the square-root relationship AND computing the new n. This is why precision improvements get progressively expensive: going from n = 100 to n = 400 halves the margin; going from n = 400 to n = 1600 halves it again. The square-root law applies to all standard error formulas in inference.

questions.prompt: Test comparing observed to expected counts.
questions.explanation: The chi-square goodness-of-fit test compares observed counts to expected counts under a hypothesized distribution. AP Statistics Unit 8 introduces this test for single categorical variables with multiple categories - testing whether the observed distribution matches a stated theoretical or proportional distribution. The 2019 FRQ tested GOF in a M&M color distribution: observed counts of 100 M&Ms compared to Mars's claimed proportions. Rubric points required (a) stating hypotheses, (b) computing expected counts using H0 proportions, (c) the chi-square statistic, (d) df, (e) P-value, and (f) contextual conclusion. The GOF test generalizes the one-proportion z-test to multi-category cases.

choices: Sum (O minus E) squared over E
questions.correctText: Sum (O minus E) squared over E
questions.explanation: The chi-square test statistic is chi-square = Sigma((O - E)^2 / E), where O is the observed count and E is the expected count in each category. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes that this statistic measures the total squared discrepancy between observed and expected, weighted by 1/E. The 2018 FRQ tested chi-square computation: for 4 categories with given O and E values, students computed chi-square step-by-step. Rubric points required correctly squaring AND dividing by E in each term. Karl Pearson developed this statistic in 1900 - one of the foundational tests of modern statistics. Larger chi-square values indicate greater discrepancy and stronger evidence against H0.

Degrees of freedom for a chi-square goodness-of-fit test is k - 1, where k is the number of categories. AP Statistics Unit 8 derives this from the constraint that observed counts must sum to n - consuming one degree of freedom. The 2019 FRQ tested df identification: for a 4-category test, df = 3. Rubric points required correctly identifying df AND using it to find the P-value from the chi-square distribution. The chi-square distribution is right-skewed for small df, becoming more symmetric as df increases - approaching Normal for very large df. Larger df makes the critical value for rejection larger.

The Large Expected Counts condition requires every expected count E_i >= 5 to use the chi-square approximation reliably. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes this condition - violated when expected counts in some categories are small. The 2018 FRQ tested condition checking: a 6-category test with expected counts of 30, 25, 20, 10, 8, 7 (all >= 5) satisfies the condition; if any cell had expected count of 3, condition is violated. Rubric points required checking ALL expected counts AND noting if any failed. When condition fails, options include combining categories, using exact methods, or collecting more data.

choices: Sum (O minus E) squared over E
questions.explanation: When the chi-square statistic is large (P-value < alpha), the conclusion is to REJECT H0 - the observed distribution differs from the hypothesized distribution. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes contextual interpretation: 'we have convincing evidence that the M&M color proportions differ from Mars's claim.' The 2019 FRQ tested this conclusion: chi-square = 13.5, df = 5, P-value < 0.05, so reject H0. Rubric points required full contextual conclusion - not just 'reject H0.' The test does NOT identify WHICH categories differ - only that the overall distribution differs. Follow-up analysis (looking at individual cell contributions) identifies the specific discrepancies.

questions.prompt: Test for association between two categorical variables.
questions.explanation: The chi-square test of independence assesses whether two categorical variables are associated in a single sample. AP Statistics Unit 8 distinguishes this from homogeneity (next category): independence tests one sample's two variables; homogeneity compares distributions across multiple samples. The 2019 FRQ tested independence in a survey of music preference (rock/jazz/classical) vs age group (teen/adult/senior). Rubric points required (a) stating H0: variables are independent, (b) expected counts via row*col/n, (c) chi-square, (d) df = (r-1)(c-1), (e) P-value, and (f) contextual conclusion. The independence test was developed by Karl Pearson in 1904.

choices: Row total times column total over n
questions.explanation: A two-way table (or contingency table) cross-classifies two categorical variables, showing the count of observations in each combination. AP Statistics Unit 8 uses two-way tables as the standard data structure for chi-square independence and homogeneity tests. The 2018 FRQ presented a 3x4 two-way table of smoking status by age group and asked students to compute expected counts. Rubric points required correctly identifying row totals, column totals, and the grand total n. Two-way tables also support conditional probability and Simpson's paradox - making them a versatile pedagogical tool throughout Units 4 and 8.

questions.prompt: Degrees of freedom for r by c table.
choices: Row total times column total over n
questions.explanation: Degrees of freedom for a chi-square independence test on an r by c table is df = (r - 1) * (c - 1). AP Statistics Unit 8 derives this from the constraint that row and column marginal totals are fixed - leaving (r - 1)*(c - 1) free cells. The 2019 FRQ tested df identification: for a 3x4 table, df = (3-1)*(4-1) = 6. Rubric points required correctly identifying df AND using it for the P-value. This formula is the same for homogeneity tests, which differ only in sampling design (not in mathematics). Larger tables (more rows or columns) yield more df, requiring larger chi-square statistics for rejection.

choices: Row total times column total over n
questions.correctText: Row total times column total over n
questions.explanation: Expected counts for chi-square tests on two-way tables are E_ij = (row_i total * col_j total) / n, where n is the grand total. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes that expected counts are what we would see IF the two variables were independent (or if the distributions were homogeneous, for homogeneity tests). The 2018 FRQ tested expected-count computation: for a cell with row total 50, column total 60, n = 200, expected count = 15. Rubric points required showing the formula AND computing all expected counts before proceeding to chi-square. Always verify expected counts before computing chi-square - the Large Expected Counts condition requires all >= 5.

The difference between the independence and homogeneity tests lies in SAMPLING DESIGN, not in the mathematics of the chi-square test. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes: independence draws a SINGLE random sample then cross-classifies two variables; homogeneity draws SEPARATE random samples from different populations or treatments. The 2019 FRQ tested design recognition: a study taking separate samples of men and women asks for homogeneity (comparing the male distribution to the female distribution), not independence. Rubric points required correctly identifying the design AND stating the appropriate H0 (variables independent vs distributions identical). The mathematics is identical; the interpretation differs.

The chi-square test of homogeneity compares the distribution of a single categorical variable across multiple populations or treatments using independent samples. AP Statistics Unit 8 distinguishes this from independence (previous category): homogeneity uses MULTIPLE samples; independence uses one. The 2018 FRQ tested homogeneity in comparing music preference across grade levels with separate samples per grade. Rubric points required (a) hypotheses about equal distributions, (b) expected counts, (c) chi-square statistic, (d) df = (r-1)(c-1), (e) P-value, and (f) contextual conclusion. The mathematics is identical to the independence test - only the interpretation differs.

choices: Row total times column total over n
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis in a chi-square homogeneity test is H0: the distributions of the categorical variable are the same across populations. AP Statistics Unit 8 contrasts this with independence's H0 (variables are independent). The 2019 FRQ tested hypothesis formulation: students were asked to write H0 in context - 'the distribution of music preferences is the same for 9th, 10th, 11th, and 12th graders.' Rubric points required correctly identifying homogeneity (multiple populations) and stating H0 in context. The alternative is Ha: at least one distribution differs from the others. The chi-square test does NOT identify WHICH distributions differ - only that they differ overall.

A stratified sampling design - separate random samples from different populations - is the typical setup for a chi-square homogeneity test. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes that this design differs from a single random sample (used in independence tests). The 2018 FRQ tested design recognition: a survey of 100 randomly chosen 9th graders, 100 randomly chosen 10th graders, etc., is stratified - so the appropriate test is homogeneity. Rubric points required citing the stratified design AND the homogeneity test as the match. Stratified samples often arise in policy and educational research where separate group comparisons are of interest.

choices: Row total times column total over n
questions.correctText: Row total times column total over n
questions.explanation: Expected counts for chi-square homogeneity tests use the same formula E_ij = row_total * column_total / grand_total - mathematically identical to independence. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes that the EXPECTED COUNT FORMULAS are the same; only the INTERPRETATION differs between independence (no association) and homogeneity (equal distributions). The 2019 FRQ tested expected-count computation in a 4-population, 3-category table: students computed all 12 expected counts before the chi-square statistic. Rubric points required correctly applying the formula AND showing the intermediate work. The numerical equivalence of the two tests is a common source of confusion.

choices: Row total times column total over n
questions.explanation: The mathematical difference between chi-square tests of independence and homogeneity is zero - same formulas, same df, same critical values. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes that the only difference is in SAMPLING DESIGN and the resulting INTERPRETATION. The 2018 FRQ tested this distinction in a tricky design problem: students who confused independence and homogeneity (stating the wrong H0) lost rubric points for design identification. Rubric points required identifying the sampling design (single sample vs stratified) AND choosing the test that matches. This is the most commonly tested 'concept' question in Unit 8.

The chi-square distribution is RIGHT-SKEWED, especially for small df. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes this asymmetry: the distribution starts at 0 (lowest value), peaks at df - 2 (for df > 2), and has a long right tail. For df = 1, the distribution is dramatically right-skewed with a peak at 0. The 2019 FRQ tested distribution shape recognition: students were given a chi-square graph and asked to identify whether the shape suggested small or large df. Rubric points required citing the right-skew property AND noting how df affects the shape. The chi-square arises as the sum of squared standard Normal variables - df of them.

The mean of the chi-square distribution is exactly df - its degrees of freedom. AP Statistics Unit 8 derives this from the chi-square as a sum of df independent squared standard Normal variables, each contributing 1 to the expectation. The 2018 FRQ tested this property: for df = 10, the expected value of the chi-square statistic is 10. Rubric points required citing df as the mean AND linking to the sum-of-squared-Normals derivation. This property gives an intuitive benchmark: a chi-square statistic far above df is evidence against H0. The variance of chi-square is 2*df.

The lowest possible chi-square value is 0 - achieved when observed counts exactly equal expected counts. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes this floor: chi-square cannot be negative because it's a sum of squared terms divided by positive expected counts. The 2019 FRQ tested boundary recognition: if observed exactly matched expected, chi-square = 0 and P-value = 1. Rubric points required citing the floor AND interpreting that 'zero chi-square means perfect fit with H0.' This contrasts with t-statistics, which can be any real number. Chi-square = 0 is the rarest outcome - almost never seen in real data due to natural variability.

questions.prompt: As df increases, chi-square shape approaches.
choices: Sum (O minus E) squared over E
questions.explanation: As degrees of freedom increase, the chi-square distribution approaches a NORMAL distribution. AP Statistics Unit 8 explains this via the Central Limit Theorem: chi-square = sum of df independent squared Normal variables, and a sum of independent variables converges to Normal by CLT. The 2018 FRQ tested this property conceptually: for df = 100, the chi-square is approximately Normal with mean 100 and SD = sqrt(200). Rubric points required citing the CLT-based limit AND the mean df and variance 2df. For df >= 30, Normal approximation is acceptable; for smaller df, the right-skew matters and chi-square tables or technology are needed.

Critical values for chi-square tests come from chi-square tables (or calculator/computer functions) - chi-square*_(alpha, df) is the value with probability alpha to the RIGHT. AP Statistics Unit 8 expects students to use the table or invChi2 function to find critical values. The 2019 FRQ tested critical-value identification: for alpha = 0.05 and df = 4, chi-square* = 9.488. Rubric points required correctly looking up the value AND comparing the observed chi-square to it. Reject H0 if chi-square > chi-square*. Chi-square is RIGHT-tailed only (unlike z and t which can be one- or two-tailed) because larger chi-square always indicates worse fit.

All three chi-square tests (GOF, independence, homogeneity) compare OBSERVED counts to EXPECTED counts. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes this common structure: chi-square = Sigma((O - E)^2 / E) is computed the same way regardless of which test. The 2019 FRQ tested test identification by sampling design and number of variables. Rubric points required identifying the test type (GOF, independence, or homogeneity) based on the data structure: one sample one variable (GOF), one sample two variables (independence), multiple samples one variable (homogeneity). The same chi-square formula then applies.

questions.prompt: Condition on counts for chi-square inference.
questions.explanation: The Large Expected Counts condition requires every expected count >= 5 across all three chi-square tests. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes this as the most commonly violated condition in real data - cells with small counts inflate chi-square arbitrarily. The 2018 FRQ tested condition checking: in a 4x5 table, all 20 expected counts must be checked. Rubric points required checking ALL cells AND citing which were below 5 if any were. When condition fails, options include (a) combining categories sensibly, (b) using exact methods (Fisher's exact test for 2x2), or (c) collecting more data. The threshold of 5 is conventional; some texts use 10 for stricter validity.

The goodness-of-fit test is the chi-square test for ONE categorical variable with multiple categories. AP Statistics Unit 8 distinguishes GOF as the simplest of the three chi-square tests - single variable, one sample. The 2019 FRQ tested GOF in a coin-fairness study: 100 coin flips with observed counts 45 heads, 55 tails, expected 50-50. Students computed chi-square = (45-50)^2/50 + (55-50)^2/50 = 1.0, df = 1, P-value ~ 0.32, so failed to reject H0 of fairness. Rubric points required identifying GOF AND showing the full procedure. GOF generalizes the one-proportion z-test to multiple categories.

questions.prompt: Test for two categorical variables in one sample.
questions.explanation: The independence test is the chi-square test for TWO categorical variables in ONE sample. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes that independence tests whether knowing one variable provides information about the other - H0: variables are independent. The 2018 FRQ tested independence in a survey of music preference (rock/jazz/classical) vs age group (teen/adult/senior) from one sample. Rubric points required identifying independence (not homogeneity) AND stating H0 in context. The mathematics is identical to homogeneity; the distinction lies in design (one sample vs multiple samples) and interpretation (no association vs equal distributions).

questions.prompt: Test for one categorical across several samples.
questions.explanation: The homogeneity test is the chi-square test for ONE categorical variable across MULTIPLE samples. AP Statistics Unit 8 emphasizes that homogeneity tests whether the distribution is the same across populations - H0: distributions are identical. The 2019 FRQ tested homogeneity in comparing music preference across grade levels with separate samples per grade. Rubric points required identifying homogeneity (not independence) AND stating H0 in context. The mathematics is identical to independence; the distinction lies in design (multiple samples vs one sample) and interpretation (equal distributions vs no association). This distinction is the most-tested concept in Unit 8.

choices: b plus or minus t star times SE sub b
questions.explanation: Slope inference is the chi-square or t-based testing of the population slope beta from the regression line y = alpha + beta*x + epsilon. AP Statistics Unit 9 introduces slope inference as the cornerstone of regression-based inference: testing whether x is statistically associated with y. The 2019 FRQ tested slope inference in a study of cricket-chirp rate vs temperature: was the slope significantly different from 0? Rubric points required (a) hypotheses about beta, (b) condition check (LINER), (c) test statistic, (d) df = n - 2, (e) P-value, and (f) contextual conclusion. Rejecting H0: beta = 0 means there IS a linear association.

The test statistic for slope inference is t = (b - beta_0) / SE_b, where b is the sample slope, beta_0 is the hypothesized value (usually 0), and SE_b is the standard error of the slope. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes that this is a one-sample t-test on the slope. The 2018 FRQ tested t computation using calculator output: students read b and SE_b from LinRegTTest output and computed t = b / SE_b. Rubric points required showing the formula AND interpreting the t-statistic in context: 'the slope is X standard errors from 0.' Larger |t| indicates stronger evidence against H0: beta = 0.

questions.prompt: Degrees of freedom for slope inference.
questions.explanation: Degrees of freedom for slope inference is n - 2, where n is the number of (x, y) data points. AP Statistics Unit 9 derives this from estimating two parameters (slope and intercept) from the data - each consumes one degree of freedom. The 2019 FRQ tested df identification: for n = 20 paired observations, df = 18. Rubric points required correctly identifying df AND using it to find the critical t* or P-value. Note this differs from one-sample t (df = n - 1) because regression estimates two parameters. As n grows, df grows and the t distribution approaches Normal.

choices: b plus or minus t star times SE sub b
questions.correctText: b plus or minus t star times SE sub b
questions.explanation: The confidence interval for the slope beta is b plus or minus t*-times-SE_b, where t* is the critical value with df = n - 2. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes this CI parallels the one-sample t CI for the mean. The 2018 FRQ tested CI construction using calculator output: b = 0.5, SE_b = 0.1, n = 20, df = 18, t* for 95 percent CI is 2.101. CI = 0.5 plus or minus 0.21 = (0.29, 0.71). Rubric points required correct t* AND contextual interpretation: 'we are 95 percent confident the population slope is between 0.29 and 0.71.' If the CI doesn't include 0, the slope is significantly different from 0.

choices: b plus or minus t star times SE sub b
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis for slope inference is H0: beta = 0 - testing whether there is no linear association between x and y. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes this is the standard null. The alternative is Ha: beta ≠ 0 (or beta > 0, beta < 0). The 2019 FRQ tested hypothesis formulation: 'H0: there is no linear association between temperature and cricket chirps' is equivalent to H0: beta = 0. Rubric points required stating H0 about beta (not b - the sample slope) AND a contextual sentence. Rejecting H0 supports the claim that x and y have a linear association in the population - one of the most useful conclusions in regression analysis.

The LINER conditions for regression inference are: Linear (relationship between x and y is linear), Independent (observations are independent), Normal (residuals are approximately Normal), Equal SD (residuals have constant variance), Random (data come from random sample or random assignment). AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes that all five conditions must be checked before applying t-based inference. The 2019 FRQ docked points for students who skipped condition verification. Rubric points required explicit checks for each of the five conditions. The acronym LINER is the standard memory aid; some texts use LINE (combining Random with Independent).

The Linear condition is checked using the SCATTERPLOT of y vs x. AP Statistics Unit 9 expects students to inspect the scatterplot for an approximately linear pattern - no obvious curvature or other systematic departures. The 2018 FRQ tested condition checking: a clearly curved scatterplot fails the Linear condition, and t-based inference is inappropriate. Rubric points required actually examining the scatterplot AND citing linearity (or its absence). When Linear fails, transformations (log, sqrt) often restore linearity, allowing inference on the transformed scale. Always pair the scatterplot check with a residual plot.

The Equal SD condition is checked using the RESIDUAL PLOT - the scatter should have constant vertical spread across all values of x. AP Statistics Unit 9 explains that this condition (homoscedasticity) is what makes the standard error formulas valid. The 2019 FRQ tested condition checking: a residual plot with a fan shape (spread increases with x) fails the Equal SD condition. Rubric points required examining the residual plot AND citing equal spread (or its absence). When Equal SD fails, transformations (log y often helps) can stabilize variance. Robust regression is an alternative but is rarely covered in AP Statistics.

The Normal condition is checked using a HISTOGRAM of residuals (or Normal probability plot of residuals). AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes that residuals - not raw y values - must be approximately Normal for t-based inference to be valid. The 2018 FRQ tested condition checking: a strongly skewed histogram of residuals fails the Normal condition. Rubric points required examining the residual histogram AND citing approximate Normality (or its absence). With large n (>= 30), the CLT provides some robustness, but heavy skew or outliers in residuals always warrants concern. Normal probability plots provide a stricter check.

Independence is checked through the SAMPLING METHOD - either random sampling from a population OR random assignment of treatments. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes that independence cannot be checked from the data alone - it must be assured by the design. The 2019 FRQ tested independence assessment: a convenience sample of friends violates the random condition and therefore independence, invalidating t inference. Rubric points required citing the sampling method AND noting that random sampling/assignment supports independence. The 10 percent rule applies for finite populations: sample size <= 10 percent of population.

questions.prompt: Computer output line for slope estimate.
questions.explanation: The slope estimate appears in computer/calculator regression output under labels like 'Coefficient,' 'Estimate,' 'B,' or simply 'b.' AP Statistics Unit 9 expects students to read b from the output, recognize that b is the LSRL slope, and interpret it in context. The 2018 FRQ tested output interpretation: 'Coefficient: 0.45' means each one-unit increase in x is predicted to increase y by 0.45. Rubric points required identifying the slope value AND interpreting in context with units. The intercept also appears in the output, typically labeled '(Intercept)' or 'a' - students must interpret it carefully when x = 0 is far from observed data.

questions.prompt: Standard error of slope in output.
questions.explanation: The standard error of the slope appears in regression output under labels like 'Std Error of Coef,' 'SE Coef,' or 'Std Error.' AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes that SE_b quantifies the precision of the slope estimate. The 2019 FRQ tested SE interpretation: 'SE Coef: 0.08' means the slope estimate has a typical variability of 0.08 units across samples. Rubric points required identifying SE_b AND using it in CI construction (b plus or minus t*-times-SE_b) or test statistic computation (t = b / SE_b). Smaller SE_b indicates more precision; SE_b shrinks with larger n and tighter scatter around the LSRL.

choices: b plus or minus t star times SE sub b
questions.explanation: The t-value in regression output is the test statistic t = b / SE_b - testing H0: beta = 0. AP Statistics Unit 9 expects students to read the t-value directly from the output rather than computing it. The 2018 FRQ tested t-value interpretation: 't-value: 5.6' means the slope is 5.6 standard errors from 0 - a strong departure from H0. Rubric points required interpreting the t-value AND comparing it to the appropriate critical value or using it to find the P-value. Larger |t| indicates stronger evidence against H0: beta = 0. The two-tailed P-value is typically reported directly.

The P-value column for the slope, often labeled 'Pr(>|t|)' or 'P-value' in regression output, gives the two-tailed P-value for H0: beta = 0. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes reading the P-value directly and using it to make a conclusion. The 2019 FRQ tested P-value interpretation: 'P-value: 0.002' means we have strong evidence against H0: beta = 0 - the population slope is significantly different from 0. Rubric points required citing the P-value AND making a conclusion with appropriate alpha level. For one-tailed tests, halve the P-value if the alternative direction is consistent with the observed slope direction.

The coefficient of determination r-squared in regression output (often labeled 'R-Squared' or 'Multiple R^2') is the proportion of variation in y explained by linear regression on x. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes interpreting r-squared in context: r-squared = 0.81 means 81 percent of variation in y is explained by the linear model on x. The 2018 FRQ tested r-squared interpretation: rubric points required all three elements - percent, variation in (response), explained by linear regression on (explanatory). r-squared is the square of correlation r - so r = sqrt(0.81) = 0.9 indicates strong positive correlation if slope is positive.

Extrapolation is using a regression model to predict y for x values OUTSIDE the observed range. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes the danger of extrapolation: the linear relationship may break down outside the observed range, producing unreliable or nonsensical predictions. The 2019 FRQ tested extrapolation recognition: using a child-height regression to predict adult height extrapolates outside the data, and the prediction is unreliable. Rubric points required citing the extrapolation AND noting it as a limitation. Always check whether predictions are within the observed x range; flag extrapolations explicitly when reporting predictions.

Interpolation is using a regression model to predict y for x values WITHIN the observed range. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes interpolation as the reliable use of regression - the model has been validated over this range. The 2018 FRQ tested interpolation vs extrapolation: predicting cricket chirps at temperatures within the observed range (60-90 F) is interpolation; predicting at 100 F is extrapolation. Rubric points required correctly classifying predictions AND explaining the reliability difference. Interpolation is the standard, reliable use of regression; extrapolation always warrants caution.

An influential point is one whose removal dramatically changes the LSRL slope, intercept, or r. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes that influence depends on BOTH high leverage (extreme x) AND a residual far from zero. The 2019 FRQ tested influence assessment: a single point far from the line dramatically changes the regression conclusions. Rubric points required identifying the influential point AND computing the regression both with and without it to demonstrate the change. The 2014 FRQ asked students to recompute the LSRL after removing a candidate influential point - the dramatic slope change confirmed influence.

Causation requires random assignment - the only design that supports causal inference from regression. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes that even a statistically significant slope in observational data does NOT establish causation: confounding variables may explain the association. The 2018 FRQ tested this in a study of coffee consumption and heart disease: a positive slope is consistent with causation but does not establish it. Rubric points required citing random assignment as the requirement AND identifying plausible confounders (e.g., smoking, age) that could explain the observed slope. The Bradford Hill criteria (1965) provide additional support for causation from observational data.

questions.prompt: Hidden variable affecting both x and y.
questions.explanation: A confounding variable is a third variable that affects BOTH the explanatory and response variables, creating a spurious association in regression. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes confounding as the central limitation of observational regression. The classic example: the positive slope between ice cream sales and drowning deaths reflects a confounder (summer temperature), not causation. The 2019 FRQ tested confounding identification in a study of fast-food consumption and obesity: rubric points were awarded for naming a plausible confounder (e.g., overall diet, exercise habits) and explaining how it could explain the observed slope. Always consider confounders before claiming causation.

questions.prompt: Way to increase power of slope test.
questions.explanation: Increasing sample size n INCREASES the power of a slope test - by reducing SE_b and making the t-statistic more extreme for the same true slope. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes this as the most reliable way to improve power. The 2018 FRQ tested power factors: a researcher with a small but real effect should plan a larger sample. Rubric points required citing sample size AND explaining the SE_b reduction with sqrt(n). Doubling n shrinks SE_b by factor sqrt(2)/2 approximately 0.707; quadrupling n halves SE_b. This sqrt(n) law underlies all power analyses in classical inference.

questions.prompt: Effect of more variability on power.
questions.explanation: More variability in y (around the regression line) DECREASES the power of a slope test - by inflating SE_b and reducing the t-statistic. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes that variability is the noise that obscures the signal (slope). The 2019 FRQ tested this: a study with extremely noisy y measurements requires very large n to achieve adequate power. Rubric points required citing variability AND its inverse relationship to power. The signal-to-noise ratio - effect size divided by SE - drives power; reducing noise has the same effect as increasing effect size.

questions.prompt: Effect of stronger effect size on power.
questions.explanation: A stronger effect size (larger true slope) INCREASES the power of a slope test. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes that effect size and sample size are the two main drivers of power: large effects are easily detected with small samples; small effects require large samples. The 2018 FRQ tested effect-size interpretation: a slope of 0.5 with SE_b = 0.1 gives t = 5, easily significant; a slope of 0.05 with SE_b = 0.1 gives t = 0.5, not significant. Rubric points required citing effect size AND its direct relationship to power. Effect sizes are context-specific; researchers must judge what magnitude is meaningful.

questions.prompt: Effect of larger alpha on power.
questions.explanation: Increasing alpha (the significance level) INCREASES the power of a slope test - by lowering the threshold for rejecting H0. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes the trade-off: larger alpha increases power but also increases Type I error. The 2019 FRQ tested this trade-off conceptually: changing alpha from 0.05 to 0.10 doubles the rejection threshold sensitivity but doubles Type I error too. Rubric points required citing the trade-off AND noting that researchers must balance the two error types. Alpha is usually chosen by convention (0.05, 0.01) and not adjusted to maximize power.

Alpha (Type I error rate) and beta (Type II error rate) have an INVERSE relationship for fixed sample size - reducing one increases the other. AP Statistics Unit 9 emphasizes this fundamental trade-off in hypothesis testing. The 2018 FRQ tested this in slope inference: requiring stronger evidence (smaller alpha) increases the chance of missing a real slope (larger beta). Rubric points required citing the trade-off AND explaining that only increasing sample size n can reduce BOTH error rates simultaneously. Modern power analyses choose n to achieve target power (often 0.80) at conventional alpha (0.05) for a planned effect size.

The median is the middle value of an ordered data set (50th percentile). Unlike the mean, the median is RESISTANT to outliers - a single extreme value does not change it. AP Statistics Unit 1 introduces the median; it appears throughout the course as the preferred center for skewed distributions. The 2019 FRQ tested median identification in income data: median, not mean, is appropriate because of right skew. Rubric points required citing the resistance property. The Tukey (1977) five-number summary places the median at Q2, with Q1 and Q3 forming the IQR. Median is the only center valid for ordinal data (e.g., satisfaction ratings).

choices: x-bar plus or minus t star times SE
questions.explanation: The interquartile range IQR = Q3 - Q1 measures the spread of the middle 50 percent of data. AP Statistics Unit 1 introduces IQR as the resistant spread measure (paired with median). IQR is computed from the five-number summary and underlies the Tukey 1.5*IQR outlier rule. The 2019 FRQ tested IQR computation and application: students computed IQR and applied the 1.5*IQR fence rule to identify outliers. Rubric points required showing the formula AND the outlier identification. IQR is preferred over SD for skewed distributions because SD is sensitive to outliers but IQR is not.

A scatterplot displays the relationship between two quantitative variables. AP Statistics Unit 2 introduces scatterplots as the foundational graph for regression - the basis for assessing the LINER conditions. The 2018 FRQ tested scatterplot interpretation in a study of cricket chirps vs temperature: students described direction, form, strength, and unusual features. Rubric points required all four elements of description. Scatterplots support the choice of regression model (linear or transformed) and the assessment of conditions for inference.

The coefficient of determination r-squared is the proportion of variation in y explained by linear regression on x. AP Statistics Unit 2 introduces r-squared as the squared correlation, ranging from 0 to 1. The 2019 FRQ tested r-squared interpretation: r-squared = 0.81 means 81 percent of variation in y is explained by linear regression on x. Rubric points required all three elements - percent, variation in y, explained by linear regression on x. The remaining 1 - r-squared is unexplained variation due to random noise plus other unmeasured variables.

A z-score z = (x - mu)/sigma standardizes an observation by expressing its distance from the mean in units of standard deviation. AP Statistics Unit 1 introduces z-scores, which are foundational throughout the course. The 2019 FRQ tested z-score comparison: SAT score 1400 (mu = 1000, sigma = 200) gives z = 2; ACT score 30 (mu = 21, sigma = 4) gives z = 2.25. The ACT score is relatively higher because its z-score is larger. Rubric points required showing both z-scores AND explicit comparison. Z-scores are unitless and enable comparison across distributions.

Probability is the long-run relative frequency of an event - the proportion of times an event would occur in a very large number of trials. AP Statistics Unit 4 introduces this frequentist interpretation. The Law of Large Numbers (Bernoulli 1713) guarantees that empirical proportions converge to true probability as trials -> infinity. The 2019 FRQ tested the frequentist interpretation - rubric points required citing 'long-run relative frequency' (not just 'frequency' or 'chance'). Subjective and Bayesian interpretations treat probability as degrees of belief.

Conditional probability P(A|B) is the probability of A given B has occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B). AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes that P(A|B) is generally NOT equal to P(B|A) - confusing the two is the 'prosecutor's fallacy.' The 2018 FRQ tested conditional probability with a two-way table: students computed P(positive test | has disease) and P(has disease | positive test), demonstrating their inequality. Rubric points required correctly identifying which probability was being computed AND showing the formula.

questions.prompt: Distribution counting successes in n trials.
questions.explanation: The binomial distribution describes the number of successes X in n independent trials, each with probability p of success. AP Statistics Unit 4 introduces the binomial as the standard distribution for fixed-trial yes/no experiments. Mean: n*p. SD: sqrt(n*p*(1-p)). The 2019 FRQ tested binomial computation: P(at least 3 successes in 10 trials, p = 0.4) - rubric points required either explicit binomial PMF summation or use of 1 - binomcdf with correct bounds. The BINS conditions (Binary, Independent, Number fixed, Same p) must hold.

The Normal distribution N(mu, sigma) is the bell-shaped continuous distribution central to AP Statistics. Carl Friedrich Gauss formalized it in 1809 to model astronomical measurement errors. AP Statistics Unit 4 emphasizes the Normal as the foundation for inference via the CLT. The 2019 FRQ tested Normal probability: using z-scores and the standard Normal table (or normalcdf), students computed probabilities for any Normal distribution. Rubric points required correct standardization step (Z = (X - mu)/sigma) AND probability lookup. The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7) provides fast approximate values.

questions.prompt: Theorem giving sample means as normal.
questions.explanation: The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that for sufficiently large n, the sampling distribution of x-bar is approximately Normal with mean mu and SD sigma/sqrt(n), regardless of population shape. AP Statistics Unit 5 emphasizes the CLT as the single most important theorem - it justifies Normal-based inference for means even when population shape is unknown. The conventional rule is n >= 30. The 2019 FRQ tested CLT recognition: students cited n >= 30 as justification for Normal inference. The CLT (Laplace 1810, de Moivre 1733) is foundational to all classical statistical inference.

questions.prompt: Sample where each has equal chance.
questions.explanation: A Simple Random Sample (SRS) is one in which every individual in the population - and every subset of size n - has an equal chance of selection. AP Statistics Unit 3 emphasizes the SRS as the gold standard against which all sampling methods are compared.
